#### Core Rules

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **d20** | The 20 sided die used for determining success or failure in most actions. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Advantage** | Roll 2d20 and take the higher result. Multiple sources stack up to triple advantage. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Disadvantage** | Roll 2d20 and take the lower result. Multiple sources stack up to triple disadvantage. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Difficulty Class (DC)** | A target number set by the GM representing how hard a task is. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Saving Throw** | A raw stat roll to resist effects such as poison or spells. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Skill Check** | A d20 roll plus attribute modifier plus proficiency bonus compared against a DC. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Contested Roll** | Two creatures roll against each other. The higher total wins. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Modifier (Stat)** | A bonus or penalty derived from a core stat. Formula: (Stat minus 10) divided by 2. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Natural 1 (Nat 1)** | The die showing 1. Results in a Critical Fail. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Natural 20 (Nat 20)** | The die showing 20. Results in a Critical Success. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Critical Fail** | Rolling a Natural 1 resulting in major negative consequences. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Critical Hit** | A Natural 20 on an attack roll or exceeding AC by 10 or more. Deals maximum damage bypassing Vitality. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Critical Success** | A Natural 20 or exceeding the DC by 10 or more. Flawless execution. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Great Success** | Meeting or exceeding DC plus 5. Grants extra benefits. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Dirty 20** | When your roll plus modifiers equals 20 without it being a Natural 20. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Stacking Effects** | Bonuses of the same type from the same source do not stack. Only the highest applies. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Action Point (Ap)** | A resource used to perform primary actions on your turn, such as attacking or casting a spell. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Bonus Action Point (BAp)** | A resource for secondary actions on your turn, such as activating a class ability. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Reaction Point (Rp)** | A resource for responding to triggers outside your turn. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Free Action** | Minor actions requiring no action points, such as speaking or dropping an item. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Initiative** | The order in which combatants act each round. Calculated as 1d20 plus Dexterity modifier. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Surprise Round** | When one side ambushes another. The ambushing party acts first before normal initiative begins. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Delaying Your Turn** | Choosing to act later in the round by lowering your initiative position permanently. Must be declared before taking any actions on your turn. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Grouped Initiative** | All monsters of the same type act on the same initiative count. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Individual Initiative** | Each combatant rolls their own initiative and acts independently. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Movement Action** | You can move up to your Speed each turn, split around actions. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Movement Speed** | How far you can move on your turn in feet. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Concentration** | Maintaining focus on a sustained spell. Taking damage triggers a Concentration Check. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Upcasting** | Casting a spell with more mana than its base cost for stronger effects. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Action** | Using an action point to perform a task such as attacking, casting a spell, or dashing. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Dash** | An action letting you move an additional distance equal to your Base Movement Speed. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Disengage** | An action preventing opportunity attacks for the rest of your turn. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Dodge** | An action granting disadvantage on attacks against you and advantage on Dexterity saves. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Hide** | An action using Stealth to become hidden. Requires light cover or darkness. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Search** | An action making a Search check to find traps or hidden creatures. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Help** | An action granting an ally advantage on their next ability check or attack. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Ready Action** | Holding an action for later when a specific trigger occurs. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Grapple** | An action to grab a creature via contested Athletics roll. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Shove** | An action to push a creature prone and 5 ft via contested Athletics. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Opportunity Attack** | A melee attack made as a Reaction when a creature leaves your reach. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Interrupt** | An ability that resolves before its trigger finishes, such as Counterspell. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Interrupting Actions** | Multiple simultaneous reactions resolve in initiative order, highest first. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Percentage Dice** | The 2d10 rolled together to generate a number from 1 to 100. Used for special tables or percentile effects. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |

#### Combat

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Armor Class (AC)** | A number representing how hard you are to hit. Base formula: 10 plus Armor plus Dexterity plus Shield plus other bonuses. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]], [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Attack Roll** | A d20 roll to determine if an attack hits the target AC. Melee uses Strength. Ranged uses Dexterity. Magical uses affinities. | [[1.4 Actions]], [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Spell Attack Roll** | A d20 roll plus Generic Affinity plus Relevant Affinity plus Base Magic Accuracy compared against target AC. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Spell Save DC** | The DC a target must beat to resist a spell. Formula: 10 plus Generic Affinity plus Relevant Affinity. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Cover** | Obstruction granting AC bonuses. Half cover grants +2 AC. Three quarters cover grants +5 AC. Total cover prevents targeting. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **High Ground** | Being at least 1 ft above a target gives you advantage on attacks against them. They have disadvantage attacking you. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Flanking** | Attacking from behind while an ally engages from the front gives advantage on the attack roll. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Damage Bypass** | Damage that goes directly to Hit Points, ignoring Vitality. Caused by critical hits and half of Area of Effect damage. | [[2.2 Damage Types]] |
| **Vitality Bypass** | Effects that skip Vitality and damage Hit Points directly. | [[2.2 Damage Types]] |
| **Ranged Attack Distances** | Normal range has no penalty. Long range (double base) imposes disadvantage. Extreme range (triple base) imposes double disadvantage. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Massive Damage** | Taking 50 percent or more of your maximum Hit Points in a single hit. Requires a Constitution save or you fall unconscious. | [[2.3 Injuries]] |
| **Fall Damage** | 1d4 bludgeoning per 5 ft fallen. You fall prone if falling more than 10 ft. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Difficult Terrain** | Ground that costs double movement to traverse, such as mud, rubble, or deep snow. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Underwater Combat** | Movement is halved without a swim speed. Melee attacks have disadvantage unless using a dagger, spear, trident, or shortsword. Ranged weapons automatically miss beyond the first range increment. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Size Categories** | Categories from Tiny to Gargantuan describing a creature's physical dimensions. Affects reach, stealth, grappling, and movement through spaces. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Light Levels** | Bright light grants full visibility. Dim light provides concealment. Darkness blocks vision without Darkvision. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Light Sources** | Torches provide 20 ft bright and 20 ft dim light for 1 hour. Lanterns provide 30 ft bright and 30 ft dim light for 5 hours with oil. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Hold Breath** | Characters can hold their breath for a number of rounds equal to 1 plus Constitution modifier (minimum 1 round). Suffocation begins after that. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Darkvision** | See in total darkness as if it were dim light, but only in shades of gray. Default range is 60 ft. | [[4.1 Races]], [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Low-Light Vision** | Treat dim light as bright light for visibility. Cannot see in complete darkness. | [[4.1 Races]] |
| **True Sight** | See everything as it truly is: invisible creatures, hidden illusions, ethereal forms, and through magical darkness. | [[1.6 Other Rules]] |
| **Turn Economy** | The system of managing Action Points, Bonus Action Points, Reaction Points, and movement each turn. | [[1.4 Actions]] |
| **Mount** | A creature ridden into battle or across terrain. Must be at least one size larger than the rider. Types include Domestic (requires handling), War (battle trained), and Exotic (magical or monstrous). | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]] |
| **Mounting and Dismounting** | Getting onto or off a mount costs 1 BAp or half your movement. You cannot mount a hostile creature without a contested Animal Handling check. | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]] |
| **Charging (Mounted)** | A mounted attack made after moving at least 30 ft directly toward a target. On hit the target must make a Strength save or be knocked prone. | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]] |
| **Companion** | A non player ally such as a familiar, summon, or tamed creature that acts on your turn. Default companion stats are 1 Ap, 1 BAp, 0 Rp. | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]], [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Familiar** | A tamed and bonded monster companion that fights alongside you and grows in power. Uses its own statblock. Requires Monster Specialist to unlock. Level cap depends on species: Slimes cap at half your level, Golems at one third, Dragons at one fifth. | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]], [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Familiar Bond** | An attunement bonus after a familiar has served for at least 3 levels. Grants +1 to all saving throws within 30 ft, the familiar gains its own Reaction Point, and +2 to its highest stat. | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]] |
| **Taming** | Acquiring a familiar requires the Monster Specialist path and a contested Animal Handling check against a willing or subdued target. Takes 10 minutes of focused effort. | [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]] |
| **Summons** | A temporary magical construct brought into battle by a spell. Acts per its spell description. Distinct from a permanent familiar or companion. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Dual Wielding** | Fighting with a weapon in each hand. Requires a Light weapon in your off-hand. Your off-hand weapon can be used for an additional attack as a Bonus Action. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Damage Types** | Broad categories of damage. Physical (slashing, bludgeoning, piercing), Elemental (Fire, Earth, Water, Air, and combinations), Magical (from magical sources), Mixed (Physical enhanced with magic), and Special (Force, Psychic, True). See [[2.2 Damage Types]] for the full list. | [[2.2 Damage Types]] |
| **Combination Damage Type** | An advanced damage type formed by merging two or more elemental or magical sources. Resistant only if resistant to all components or the combination specifically. | [[2.2 Damage Types]] |
| **Resistance** | Damage reduction applied in order: Immunity (none), Greater Resistance (one third), Resistance (half), Lesser Resistance (three quarters). | [[2.2 Damage Types]] |
| **Weakness** | Damage amplification applied in order: Lesser Weakness (one and a half times), Weakness (double), Greater Weakness (triple). | [[2.2 Damage Types]] |

#### Equipment

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Weapon Types** | Categories defining weapon function. See [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] for the full list. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Armor** | Protective gear categorized as Light, Medium, or Heavy. Each tier requires proficiency to use without penalties. Higher tiers grant more AC but impose speed penalties and stealth disadvantages. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Shield** | A defensive item held in one hand granting an AC bonus. Types include Buckler, Small, Medium, Large, Tower, Aspis, Kite, Heater, Pavise, Bouche, Targe, and Throwing. Cannot be used with two handed weapons. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Tool** | Equipment used for specific tasks such as lockpicking, healing, or cooking. Grants a bonus to relevant checks if proficient. | [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]], [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Material Modifiers** | Materials add bonuses to Damage, Accuracy, AC, and Tool Modifier. See [[2.6 Materials]] for the full list. | [[2.6 Materials]] |
| **Enchantability** | A material property that subtracts from the rune inscription DC. Higher values make rune inscription easier. | [[2.6 Materials]] |
| **Money** | Currency tiers from Copper to Nerite. See [[2.6 Materials]] for conversion rates. | [[2.6 Materials]] |

#### Character, Skills, and Progression

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Strength** | Core stat governing raw physical power. Modifier used for melee attacks, Athletics, and Strength saving throws. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Dexterity** | Core stat governing agility and precision. Modifier used for ranged attacks, finesse weapons, initiative, AC bonus, and Dexterity saving throws. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Constitution** | Core stat governing toughness and endurance. Modifier used for Vitality rolls, concentration checks, and Constitution saving throws. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Intelligence** | Core stat governing knowledge and reasoning. Modifier used for Spot, Nature, Religion, Medicine, and Intelligence saving throws. Determines bonus languages. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Wisdom** | Core stat governing perception and intuition. Modifier used for Mana rolls, Search, Arcana, History, and Wisdom saving throws. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Charisma** | Core stat governing social presence. Modifier used for Deception, Intimidation, Performance, Persuasion, and Charisma saving throws. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Stat Point (Stp)** | Points used to increase your six core stats. You start with 24 at level 1. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Stat Point Cost Progression** | Stats cost 1 point each from 0 to 20, 2 points each from 21 to 30, 3 points each from 31 to 40, and so on. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Skill Point (Skp)** | Points used to increase skill modifiers. You start with 5 at level 1. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Skill Point Cost Progression** | Cost depends on the skill's current value and proficiency status. Ranges from 1 point for low values with proficiency to 18 points for high values without proficiency. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Skills** | Trained capabilities tied to an attribute. Athletics (Str), Acrobatics (Dex), Sleight of Hand (Dex), Stealth (Dex), Spot (Int), Search (Wis), Arcana (Wis), History (Wis), Nature (Int), Religion (Int), Medicine (Int), Situational Insight (Wis), Social Insight (Cha), Deception (Cha), Intimidation (Cha), Performance (Cha), Persuasion (Cha), Barter (Cha). | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Saving Throw** | A raw stat roll (no skill proficiency) to resist effects. Six types matching the six core stats. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Proficiency** | Training in a skill, weapon type, or armor type. Adds your Proficiency Bonus to relevant checks. Without proficiency you cannot add your stat modifier to weapon attacks or gain full armor benefits. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]], [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Proficiency Bonus** | A scaling bonus increasing every 3 levels from +1 to +3. Added to skills and gear you are proficient in. | [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Expertise** | True mastery in a skill, weapon, or armor. For skills grants Advantage on checks. For specific weapons grants Advantage on attack rolls. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]], [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] |
| **Background** | Your character's life before the campaign. Grants advantage on tasks your character would logically excel at due to past experience. Options include Acolyte, Criminal, Soldier, Scholar, Artisan, Noble, Hermit, Urchin, Sailor, and Laborer. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]], [[3.2 Character Information]] |
| **Base Accuracy** | A class based accuracy bonus for melee, ranged, or magic attacks. | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Base Damage** | A class based damage bonus divided into Melee, Ranged, and Magic categories. Increases primarily through class levels. | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Feat** | A special ability gained when leveling up every 3 levels. Types include Passive (always active), Conditional (active under specific conditions), and Active (requires Ap, BAp, or Rp to use). | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Title** | A mark of reputation earned through significant achievements. Grants bonuses determined by the GM. Can be Self Assigned or God Granted. Multiple titles can be held simultaneously. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]], [[3.2 Character Information]] |
| **Inspiration** | A reward the GM grants for exceptional roleplay or creativity. Can be spent for advantage on any d20 roll. | [[1.7 Actually Playing The Game]] |
| **Alignment** | A label derived from your Karma score. Good (250 to 500), Neutral (249 to 249), Evil (500 to 250). | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Karma** | A numerical measure of moral standing ranging from 500 to 500. Determines your Alignment. Changed by morally significant actions. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]], [[3.2 Character Information]] |
| **Going Past Limits** | Rules for converting excess gains. Extra spell learning becomes +1 Affinity Point. Duplicate proficiency becomes 3 Skill Points. Duplicate expertise becomes 5 Skill Points. | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Skill Tree** | A branching character progression system. Every 2 levels you earn a Skill Tree Level to unlock Paths and Archetypes. A Path is a broad category (Physical, Magical, Utility, Pact, or Racial). An Archetype is a specific specialization within a Path (such as The Marksman or The Healer). Progression within an Archetype is linear and you can take Sub Levels as optional branches. | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Skill Tree Level** | A point earned every 2 levels (and 1 at level 1) used to unlock or progress in a Path or Archetype. | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Archetype** | A specific specialization within a skill tree Path (e.g., The Marksman within Physical Path). | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Sub Level** | Optional branch levels in skill trees (e.g., Level 1.5, 2.5) that do not require the next whole level to take. They provide extra bonuses like repeatable stat increases. | [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Bloodline Evolution** | A skill tree path within the Racial Path for unlocking higher tier racial abilities up to Tier 10. Requires at least 1 level in Racial Path first. | [[4.1 Races]], [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Race** | The 12 playable species of Eyum. See [[4.1 Races]] for the full list and their abilities. Race determines your starting affinities, stat bonuses, and racial abilities. | [[4.1 Races]], [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Racial Abilities (Tiers)** | Special abilities granted by your race, unlocked through the Racial Path skill tree. Tier 1 is available at level 1. Each subsequent tier requires a level in Bloodline Evolution. Goes up to Tier 10. | [[4.1 Races]] |
| **Language** | Spoken communication systems. Common is the default trade tongue spoken by nearly all civilized creatures. Racial languages include Bugish, Infernal, Elvish, Fishish, Harpy, Ophedian, Therian, Tullish, and Dead Speak. See [[4.2 Languages]] for full details. | [[4.2 Languages]], [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |

#### Resources

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Vitality (Vit)** | Your stamina and evasive buffer. Most damage is taken from Vitality first. When Vitality reaches 0, subsequent damage goes to Health. Restored on a short rest. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Health (HP)** | Your actual life force. When Health reaches 0, you die. Health damage represents real injury. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Mana (MP)** | Magical energy used to cast spells. Restored on rest. Starting die is 1d6. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Hit Dice** | Dice rolled to determine starting and per level Vitality, Health, and Mana. Your class determines your Hit Dice size. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Exhaustion** | A cumulative penalty from overexertion, lack of rest, or magical effects. Has 6 levels ranging from disadvantage on ability checks to death. Removed 1 level per long rest. | [[2.4 Conditions]] |
| **Long Rest** | 8 hours of uninterrupted rest. Fully restores Health, Vitality, and Mana. Clears all exhaustion. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Short Rest** | 30 minutes of downtime. Regains all Vitality and 50 percent of max Mana. Does not restore Health. | [[1.3 Player Stats]] |
| **Condition** | Temporary states applied to creatures across four categories: Environmental (e.g., On Fire, Soaked, Prone), Physical (e.g., Bleeding, Poisoned, Paralyzed), Mental (e.g., Charmed, Frightened, Demoralized), and Magical (e.g., Cursed, Hasted, Blessed). See [[2.4 Conditions]] for the full list. | [[2.4 Conditions]] |
| **Injury** | A negative condition applied when you fail a Constitution saving throw after taking Health damage. Severity depends on damage taken. Minor (10 to 24 percent max HP lost), Moderate (25 to 49 percent), Severe (50 percent or more), and Extreme (beyond that). | [[2.3 Injuries]] |
| **Overkill** | A scaling penalty that increases each time you succeed on an Injury Save. Makes subsequent injury saves harder. Resets when you fail an injury save. | [[2.3 Injuries]] |

#### Magic and Spells

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Generic Affinity** | Your baseline magical talent applying to all spells equally. Cannot be increased with Affinity Points. Gained from class features, race bonuses, and feats. | [[2.1 Affinities]] |
| **Specific Affinity** | Attunement to a particular element, force, or domain. Increased with Affinity Points. Higher affinity means stronger damage, higher save DCs, and access to more advanced spells. | [[2.1 Affinities]] |
| **Affinity Prerequisites** | Most affinities require minimum total points in other affinities before they can be unlocked. Fire, Earth, Water, and Air are the four basic elements accessible to all. Radiant and Necrotic each require 3 points in any of the four elements. Psychic requires 3 Radiant plus 3 Necrotic. Advanced affinities like Lightning (10 Fire plus 10 Air) require much higher investment. See [[2.1 Affinities]] for the full table. | [[2.1 Affinities]] |
| **Affinity Point (Affp)** | Points used to increase specific affinities. You start with 5 at level 1. Cannot be used on Generic Affinity. | [[3.1 Building Your Character]] |
| **Area of Effect (AoE)** | Spells or effects that hit an area rather than a single target. Half of Area of Effect damage bypasses Vitality, hitting Health directly. | [[6.1 Spells]], [[1.2 Dice and Skills]] |
| **Mana Blast** | A basic spell attack granted by the Magical Path. Fires a bolt of raw magical energy using Generic Affinity for the attack roll. | [[6.1 Spells]], [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Spell Attack Modifier** | Bonus added to the d20 for spell attacks. Formula: Generic Affinity plus half Relevant Affinity plus Base Magic Accuracy. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Spell Attack Damage** | Total damage from a spell. Formula: Specified Damage plus Base Magic Damage. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Leveled Spells** | Spells organized in progressive tiers with increasing mana cost, damage, range, and prerequisites. Each tier upgrades the previous form. A spell learned at its base tier can be upcast at higher tiers without needing to learn each form separately. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Unique Spells** | A category of spells with standalone effects that do not follow the standard level progression. Each is learned individually. | [[6.1 Spells]] |
| **Healing Spells** | A special school of spells known by any creature with levels in The Healer. Heals allies but deals damage equal to the healing amount to undead. Using Hallowed instead of Generic affinity triples the damage against undead. | [[6.1 Spells]], [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Rune** | Ancient symbols etched into items to tap into fundamental forces. Two types: Xeran for permanent enchantments and Vindroth for programmable spells with conditional logic. | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Xeran Rune** | A rune used for permanent enchantments on items. Once applied the item is permanently changed. | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Vindroth Rune** | A rune used for complex programmable spellcasting using conditional logic such as If/Then/Else. | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Runic Circle** | A binding structure for multiple effect runes on one item. Must include Carbon Runes as stabilizers. Maximum of 10 effect runes. | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Effect Rune** | A rune that provides a magical effect, as opposed to a Carbon Rune which only stabilizes. | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Carbon Rune** | A stabilizer rune with no magical effect, required to balance volatile energies in a Runic Circle. Carbon Runes must equal Effect Runes minus 1 in any circle (the Carbon Ratio). | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Inscription Check** | A d20 roll plus Relevant Affinity Modifier against a DC of 12 plus the rune's DC Mod. Required for each rune in a circle. Failure ruins the entire circle and leaves the item Saturated with unstable mana until Desaturated. | [[6.2 Runes]] |
| **Mana Surcharge** | Additional mana cost added to each rune in a Runic Circle during inscription based on the total circle size. | [[6.2 Runes]] |

#### Deities and Pacts

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Deity** | Vast celestial beings that appear as planets, stars, or moons. Three types: Gods (generally good), Fiends (generally evil), and Ancients (neutral eldest beings). Each has a Celestial Body as its physical form. | [[5.1 Deities and Pacts]] |
| **Celestial Body** | The planetary or stellar form of a deity. The deity is the celestial body. They cannot be destroyed by any known means. | [[5.1 Deities and Pacts]] |
| **Deity Ranks** | The hierarchy of deity power. The King (Darwin), Great Sovereigns (Ignivar, Aegirion, Da Vinci, Eldathis, Black Mercy, Iron Pit), Cosmic Law (Newton, Indravajra, and others), Major Forces of Nature, and Lesser Deities. Higher ranks grant greater power and access to higher pact tiers. | [[5.1 Deities and Pacts]] |
| **Pact** | A binding agreement between a character and a deity, fiend, or ancient. Each pact has four parts: The Gain (benefit), The Sacrifice (cost to form), The Time Frame (duration), and The Punishment (consequence for failure). | [[5.1 Deities and Pacts]], [[3.3 Leveling Up]] |
| **Pact Stone** | A physical object used to form a pact with a deity. Types include Godly Pact Stone (found in temples), Fiendish Pact Stone (acquired through cults or black markets), and Ancient Pact Stone (recovered from ruins). | [[5.1 Deities and Pacts]] |
| **Alignment Requirement** | Some deities require a specific Karma based alignment to form pacts. Good requires 250 to 500 Karma. Evil requires 500 to 250. | [[5.1 Deities and Pacts]] |

#### Monsters

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Monster Affinity** | A monster's primary damage type applied to all its attacks. Also grants Resistance to that damage type. | [[7.1 Monsters]] |
| **Monster Level** | Monsters can have levels that increase their power. Each level provides +1 Hit Die, +1 Generic Damage Mod, +1 AC, and +1 to all stats. | [[7.1 Monsters]] |
| **Monster Modifiers** | Templates applied to any base monster to alter its stats and abilities. Types include Strong, Fast, Tough, Smart, Savage, Large, Small, Eldritch, Veteran, and the undead modifiers Zombie and Ghost. Cannot stack with other modifiers. | [[7.3.5 Monster Modifiers]] |
| **Monster Drops** | Harvestable materials from slain monsters. See [[2.6 Materials]] for drop tables. | [[2.6 Materials]] |
| **Generic Damage Mod (Monster)** | Bonus damage added to all damage a monster deals. Typically the creature's Affinity damage type. Scales with monster level. | [[7.1 Monsters]] |
| **Difficulty Rating (DR)** | An estimate of how many level 5 players are required to defeat a single monster instance. DR 1 equals one level 5 player. DR 1/5 is closer to a level 1 player. | [[7.1 Monsters]] |
| **Universal Actives (Monster)** | Active abilities that can appear on any monster statblock. See [[7.2 Passives and Actives List]] for the full list. | [[7.2 Passives and Actives List]] |
| **Universal Passives (Monster)** | Passive abilities that can appear on any monster statblock. See [[7.2 Passives and Actives List]] for the full list. | [[7.2 Passives and Actives List]] |
| **Combat Roles** | A monster's tactical function in an encounter. Berserker frenzies attacks, Tanker absorbs hits, Guerrilla strikes and hides, Ranger attacks from range, Stealth ambushes, Mixed uses melee and range, Support buffs allies, Swarmer overwhelms with numbers. | [[7.1 Monsters]] |
| **Slime** | An elemental organism of semi liquid mass bound around a central core. Has no fixed anatomy. Drops collectible mass in jars. Universal passives include Slime Regeneration (core survives separately from body), Affinity Body (heals from its own element), and Scratch Resistance. | [[7.3.1 Slimes]] |
| **Golem** | An elemental construct of dense, solidified material bound around a single animating core. Rigid, durable, humanoid form. Role is typically Tanker. Universal passives include Golem Body (Vitality and Health split between body and core), Material Regeneration, and Golem Overcharge. | [[7.3.2 Golems]] |
| **Dragon** | A large winged reptilian creature made of biological tissue suffused with elemental energy. Fully organic with elemental infusion. Universal passives include Elemental Aura (damage per turn in radius), Flight, Elemental Form (immunity to own element), and Dragon Vitality (cannot take Health damage until Vitality is exhausted). | [[7.3.3 Dragons]] |
| **Chimera** | A hybrid animal hatched from an egg, its body formed from two or more different creatures. Includes griffins, hydras, and sphinxes. Levels determined by age rather than traditional monster leveling. | [[7.3.4 Chimera]] |

#### Meta

| Term | Definition | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Game Master (GM)** | The person who describes the world, portrays NPCs, and acts as rules referee. Also called the Narrator. | [[1.1 Introduction and Basics]] |
| **Player Character (PC)** | A character controlled by one of the players at the table. | [[1.1 Introduction and Basics]] |
| **Non-Player Character (NPC)** | A character controlled by the Game Master, including allies, enemies, and neutral figures. | [[1.1 Introduction and Basics]] |
| **Campaign** | A series of connected gaming sessions that tell a long, evolving story. | [[1.1 Introduction and Basics]] |
| **Session Zero** | A meeting before the game begins where everyone agrees on tone, rules, and content boundaries. | [[1.1 Introduction and Basics]] |
| **One-Shot** | A complete story played in a single gaming session. | [[1.1 Introduction and Basics]] |

========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.1 Introduction and Basics.md
========================================================================

# The Basics
Welcome to The World of Eyum. Whether you are a veteran of other systems or have never touched a polyhedral die in your life, this guide is designed to get you playing quickly. Eyum is a Tabletop Roleplaying Game (TTRPG).  At its core, a TTRPG is collaborative storytelling. It’s a game you play with friends to create a shared narrative. If you can imagine an action, you can attempt it (although you may not succeed). The idea is simple, it's like playing imagination games with your friends but with a set of rules to follow so things can be more fair. 

To get started simply read through the handbook in order and it will talk you through everything you need to know. If you already have an idea of how to play the game simply use the section headings to jump around. 
#### The Roles at the Table
- **The Players:** Most people at the table are Players. You each control a single Player Character (PC). You decide what they say, how they feel, and what actions they take. Your character’s details are tracked on a Character Sheet.

- The Narrator / Game Master (GM): One person takes on this role. They don’t "play against" the players. Instead, they describe the world, portray the people you meet (Non-Player Characters or NPCs), and act as the referee for the rules.
#### How Play Works
The game follows a simple loop:
1. The GM describes the scene. ("You stand before the heavy iron doors of the tomb.")
2. The Players describe what they do. ("I want to check the lock for traps.")
3. The GM determines the outcome. For most actions the GM will ask you to roll dice to see if you succeed.
#### Core Terms to Know
If you’re new, these terms will appear frequently throughout this handbook:
- **Campaign:** A series of connected sessions that tell a long, evolving story.    
- **One-Shot:** A complete story played in a single sitting.
- **D20:** A 20-sided die. This is the primary tool used to determine success or failure.
- **Difficulty Class (DC):** A number set by the GM representing how hard a task is. To succeed, your roll (plus any bonuses) must meet or beat this number.
- **Session Zero:** A meeting before the game begins where everyone agrees on the tone, rules, and character connections.
- **Race:** The species of your character, things like human, elf, or dwarf. 

For more vocab see the [[0.1 Glossary]].
#### Reading Dice Notation
We use a specific shorthand to tell you which dice to roll. It follows the format: 

{Number of Dice} d {Number of Sides}

- **1d20:** Roll one 20-sided die.
- **2d6:** Roll two 6-sided dice and add them together.
- **1d8 + 3:** Roll one 8-sided die and add 3 to the result.
# Quick Start
If you already generally know the rules then you can skip straight to character creation (see [[3.1 Building Your Character]])


========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.2 Dice and Skills.md
========================================================================

## Dice
In Eyum you will need a standard dice set (1d4, 1d6, 1d8, 2d10, 1d12, 1d20). In general your d20 (the 20 sided dice) will be used for most everything and the other dice will mostly be used for damage numbers. The 2d10 are percentage dice which have special rules. 
## The Difficulty Class (DC)
The **DC** is the target number set by the Game Master (GM) to represent how hard a task is. To succeed, your total roll must **meet or exceed** the DC.

In Eyum, your level of success is determined by how far you exceed the target:

| **Result**          | **Outcome**      | **Effect**                                                               |
| ------------------- | ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Natural 1           | Critical Fail    | You fail in the worst way possible; major negative consequences.         |
| Roll < DC           | Failure          | No positive effect. If you miss by 5+, the failure is exceptionally bad. |
| Roll ≥ DC           | Success          | You achieve your intended effect.                                        |
| Roll ≥ DC +5        | Great Success    | You succeed exceptionally well; potentially gain extra benefits.         |
| Natural 20 / DC +10 | Critical Success | Flawless execution. In combat, you always hit and deal maximum damage.   |
#### Resolving Ties
- **Standard Rolls:** If you meet the DC exactly, you succeed.
- **Contested Rolls:** If you and an opponent roll the same total, the person with the higher relevant Attribute Modifier wins. If those are equal, reroll.
### Modifiers, Proficiency, and Expertise
Rarely will you roll a d20 alone. Your character’s unique training and physical traits change your odds:
- **Ability Modifiers:** Bonuses (or penalties) derived from your core attributes (e.g., a +3 Strength modifier).
- **Proficiency Bonus:** A scaling bonus (usually +1 to +3) added to skills or gear you are trained in.
    - **Skills:** Add both your Ability Modifier and Proficiency Bonus.
    - **Weapons:** Proficiency allows you to add your Strength/Dexterity modifier to the roll. Without it, you only roll the die and cannot use the weapon's special effects.
    - **Armor:** You must be proficient to gain the full defensive benefits without penalty.
- **Expertise:** Represents true mastery. Instead of a flat bonus, Expertise grants **Advantage** (roll 2 dice, take the higher) on related rolls.

> For more information on Skills see below.
> For more information on Weapons and Armor see [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]].
#### Critical Outcomes
Rolling a Natural 20 (the number on the die face) is a special event. Roll another d20 and add it to the 20 as well as your relevant modifiers. If you roll a 20 again you continue to repeat adding all of the numbers to get a super high roll. 
- **Combat Crits**: When you crit on an attack (roll a nat 20 on an attack roll or get 10 above their armor class), you ignore the enemy's vitality and deal maximum possible damage automatically. If your attack is 1d8+4, a crit deals 12 damage, no rolling for damage required.
## Rerolls/Advantage
When you are allowed to reroll a die (usually from advantage or disadvantage), roll multiple dice and use only the highest single result (for advantage) or the lowest (for disadvantage). advantage and disadvantage only effects d20 rolls, or rolls using 20-Sided Dice. This is for things like skill checks, attack rolls and saving throws. You will never have advantage or disadvantage on damage die or anything else of the sort. You may **not** choose to ignore a reroll once made.

If you have multiple sources of advantage or disadvantage, they interact as follows:
- Each instance of advantage or disadvantage adds one “point” in that direction.
- They cancel each other out one for one.
- You may never have more than Triple advantage/disadvantage (4 dice at once)

Example: If you have 3 sources of advantage and 2 sources of disadvantage, you roll with 1 net advantage.

If your final total is:
- 1+ Advantage: Roll multiple d20s equal to your remaining advantage (up to 3 max), and take the highest.
- 1+ Disadvantage: Roll multiple d20s equal to your remaining disadvantage (up to 3 max), and take the lowest.
- 0 (Neutral): Roll 1d20 as normal.

Note: You can never roll more than 3 dice for a single advantage/disadvantage roll, even if you have more stacked. Any excess is ignored.

> **How advantage and disadvantage actually play out at the table**
>
> Let's say you're attacking from high ground (advantage), while an ally is flanking the same target (another advantage), and you have expertise in your weapon (a third advantage). Three sources of advantage, nice. But the target is in dim light (disadvantage) and you're using a ranged weapon while an enemy is within 5 ft of you (another disadvantage). That's two sources of disadvantage.
>
> Three advantage minus two disadvantage = one net advantage. You roll 2d20 and take the highest. Simple.
>
> If you somehow stacked four sources of advantage (say you also had Inspiration), you'd still cap at triple advantage (3 dice). The fourth is ignored. Same goes for disadvantage, you never roll more than 3 dice, no matter how bad the situation is.
>
> One more thing: advantage and disadvantage only affect d20 rolls, attacks, skills, saves. Never damage dice. You don't get to roll your 1d8 sword damage twice and pick the higher number. Just the d20 (other skills may grant a similar effect but it doesn't fall under advantage).

## Skill Checks
When your character attempts a specific action that has a chance of failure, like climbing, searching a room, or persuading an NPC, the GM may call for a skill check. This is typically a roll of a d20 plus the relevant attribute modifier and any proficiency bonuses you may have. The GM sets the Difficulty Class (DC), and if your total roll equals or exceeds the DC, you succeed.
###### Requesting a Check
You can always ask to make a different kind of skill check if you feel another skill better fits your approach. Likewise, you can describe your actions and ask if they require a roll, the GM will tell you which skill applies. Remember that the Game Master (GM) always gets the final say, if they don't allow you to choose a different skill, you must abide by their ruling. 
###### Alternative Modifiers
Most skills are tied to a specific attribute, but the GM may allow alternative modifiers in special cases. For instance, Intimidation usually uses Charisma, but if you’re trying to intimidate someone through a display of raw physical power (like lifting a boulder or breaking a weapon), the GM might let you roll Strength instead. These substitutions must be justified by your character’s described actions.
###### Saving Throws
Some checks do not have a specific skills associated with them, usually these are specific effects from a skill or spell and it will specify what kind of throw to make. A saving throw just uses your raw stat modifier and can't gain skill points like a normal skill. 
##### List of Skills by Attribute
###### Strength 
How strong you are
- Strength Saving Throw: resist things with your own strength 
- Athletics: Grapple, resist grapples, shoving, etc
###### Constitution
How well you can take a hit
- Constitution Saving Throw: Resisting poison, etc
###### Dexterity
How agile you are 
- Dexterity Saving Throw: Dodge out of the way, etc
- Acrobatics: General athletic abilities tied to flexibility and speed more than strength
- Sleight of Hand: General ability to use your hands for delicate tasks
- Stealth: Avoiding detection by staying unseen or unheard
###### Wisdom
How well you can use your knowledge 
- Wisdom Saving throw: Avoid mental effects
- Arcana: Your general knowledge of magic
- History: Your memory/general knowledge of the world
- Search: Your ability to find things
- Situational Insight: Your ability to read in between the lines of a situation to figure things out
###### Intelligence
How easily you gain knowledge 
- Intelligence Saving Throw: Avoid mental effects
- Spot: Your ability to notice things
- Nature: Your knowledge of nature and ability to interact with animals and nature
- Religion: Your knowledge of religion/religious practices
- Medicine: Your ability and understanding of medicine 
###### Charisma
How good you are with people 
- Charisma Saving Throw: Resist other's charm
- Deception: How good you are at lying to people
- Intimidation: How good you are at scaring people
- Performance: Your ability in music and art
- Persuasion: Your ability to convince people
- Social Insight: Your ability to read between the lines in a social situation to understand true intent 
- Barter: Your ability to barter with people
## Attack Rolls
When making an attack, whether melee, ranged, or magical, you must roll an attack roll, which is compared to the target’s Armor Class (AC). If your total attack roll equals or exceeds their AC, the attack hits. For more information on Armor Class see [[1.3 Player Stats]].

How to Calculate an Attack Roll (add all points together):

Roll a d20 then add the following to your roll;

Melee or Ranged Attack:
- Strength or Dexterity Modifier (Conditional)
    - You do not add this bonus if you are not proficient with the weapon
    - Some weapon types list which modifier to use (for more information see [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]]), otherwise:
	    - Strength Modifier is used when making general melee attacks (e.g., swinging a sword or hammer).
	    - Dexterity Modifier is used when you're targeting a specific spot (e.g., aiming for a weak point) or using a ranged attack.
- Base Accuracy is determined by your class.
- Weapon Accuracy is listed in the weapon's description (e.g., “+3 to hit”).
- Other Modifiers may come from feats, spells, conditions, etc.

Magical Attack:
- Affinity Modifier from the Spell's Type
- Generic Affinity Modifier
- Spellcasting Modifier (based on your class: Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma)
### Damage Calculation
When an attack hits, you must calculate the total damage dealt. Damage is determined differently depending on whether it is a weapon attack or a spell, but all damage is taken from the target’s Vitality first, and then Health once Vitality reaches 0.

Health and Vitality will be explained in detail in a later section. For now, understand that Vitality (Vit) represents your stamina and energy. Losing Vitality indicates the effort it takes to avoid or mitigate attacks. Health (HP) represents your actual life force. Taking Health damage is far more dangerous, as it reflects real injury rather than spent energy. For more information on Health and Vitality see [[1.3 Player Stats]]. 
###### Weapon Damage
To calculate damage from a melee or ranged weapon:
Damage = Weapon’s Damage Dice + Relevant Stat Modifier (Strength or Dexterity) + Base Damage
- Strength is used for raw force-based attacks (e.g., swinging a hammer or bashing with a shield).
- Dexterity is used when precision is the goal (e.g., aiming for a weak spot or throwing a knife).
- Your Base Damage increases primarily through your class, and sometimes through racial traits or other features. Base Damage is divided into three categories: Melee, Ranged, and Magic. When making an attack, use the Base Damage modifier that corresponds to the type of attack (melee, ranged, or magic) being used.

Examples:
- A sword that deals 1d8 damage, swung with a Strength modifier of +3 = 1d8 + 3 damage.
- A dagger thrown with a Dexterity modifier of +2 and a +1 damage bonus from enchantment = 1d4 + 2 + 1 damage.

Note: You can choose to use Dexterity or Strength on an attack by attack basis and dealing damage to a specific part of the an enemy using dexterity may invoke disadvantage if it is harder to hit but may have special effects like blinding the creature if it deals damage to Health (once Vitality is drained). This is done at the Game Masters discretion. 
###### Spell Damage
To calculate damage from a spell:
Damage = Spell’s Base Damage Dice (In the spell description) + Base Damage

For the most part the spells will specify how the damage is dealt. 
###### Area-of-Effect (AoE) Damage
When using an AoE spell or item:
- Half of the damage bypasses Vitality and hits HP directly unless otherwise stated in the spell or item description.
###### Other Ways to Bypass Vitality
While most damage is applied to Vitality first, certain situations allow damage to bypass Vitality and affect HP directly.
These include:
- Crits (Nat 20), A critical hit will always bypass Vitality and go directly to HP.
- Attacking a Sleeping or Unconscious Creature, Any successful attack against a creature that is asleep or unconscious is treated as a critical hit and bypasses Vitality, applying full damage directly to HP.
- Undodgeable or Inescapable Damage, If a creature is struck in a situation where evasion is physically impossible, such as being restrained, unaware, or helpless, then Vitality is bypassed. The Game Master (GM) determines whether an attack qualifies as truly unavoidable.    

Critical Hits always deal the maximum possible damage from the attack as well as bypassing Vitality. 

Examples:
- A sword that deals 1d8 + 4 on hit would deal 8 + 4 = 12 damage on a crit.
- A fire spell that deals 3d10 + 6 would deal 30 + 6 = 36 damage on a crit.

Note: Some abilities, weapons, or spells may still have their own critical hit enhancements. These are applied in addition to the max-damage rule unless stated otherwise.

> **Let's walk through a full attack**
>
> Say you're a level 3 Duelist swinging a longsword with 16 Strength (+3 mod). You're proficient with versatile weapons, so you add your Strength to the attack roll.
>
> **Step 1: Attack roll:** You roll a 12 on the d20, add +3 (Strength), +1 (Base Melee Accuracy from Duelist), and the longsword has no built-in accuracy bonus. Total: 12 + 3 + 1 = 16. The enemy's AC is 14. That's a hit.
>
> **Step 2: Damage:** The longsword deals 1d8 slashing. You roll a 5, add +3 (Strength), +0 (Base Melee Damage at level 3). That's 5 + 3 = 8 slashing damage. It goes to the enemy's Vitality first.
>
> **Step 3: If it were a crit:** On a natural 20, you'd deal max possible damage (8 + 3 = 11) and it would bypass Vitality, going straight to HP. The enemy would be hurting bad.
>
> **Step 4: If it were a spell:** Say you cast Firebolt instead. Your attack roll would use your Generic Affinity + Fire Affinity + Base Magic Accuracy. On hit, the damage is whatever the spell says plus your Base Magic Damage. Same Vitality-first rule applies unless you crit or hit an AoE.





========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.3 Player Stats.md
========================================================================

## Health and Vitality
Hit Points (HP) are your actual health. When HP hits 0, you die (see exceptions below). HP is harder to recover and generally requires healing magic, potions, or rest. Think of HP like “death saves without the randomness”: once you’re taking HP damage, you are actively dying and should disengage or get healed.

Vitality (Vit) is your stamina and evasive buffer, it represents dodges, deflections, and near-misses. Most damage is taken from Vitality first; when Vitality reaches 0, subsequent damage spills into HP. Vitality is easier to restore via rest, potions, or abilities, and the system treats it as a resource you can spend without long-term harm.

Generally Speaking all damage is reduced from the target’s Vitality first, and then Health if Vitality reaches 0.
##### How damage interacts with Vitality vs. HP
The normal case
- Single-target weapon/spell damage: reduce Vitality first, then HP if Vitality is at 0.
	- After taking HP damage (not Vitality damage) you run the risk of injuries.
- Area-of-Effect (AoE)
	- Unless a spell/ability says otherwise, half of the AoE’s damage bypasses Vitality and hits HP directly; the other half applies to Vitality.
- Other ways damage can bypass Vitality
	- Sleeping/Unconscious target: any successful hit is a critical and bypasses Vitality, applying full damage to HP.
	- Undodgeable/inescapable situations (restrained, helpless, truly unaware): bypass Vitality; the GM judges whether the situation was genuinely unavoidable.
	- Critical hits set all damage dice to their maximum before adding modifiers and bypasses Vitality.
##### Death and Revival
At 0 HP, a creature dies instantly.
Exceptions:  
- Some feats/features can override death at 0 HP.  
- Some spells/abilities include a revival window (e.g., “revivable within 10 minutes”). If used in time, the creature can return.
Finality: after 48 hours dead, the soul is unreachable, no magic, ritual, or divine act can restore the creature.

> **So when does damage actually hurt you?**
>
> Here's how to think about Vitality vs HP. Vitality is your stamina, dodging, blocking, rolling with the punches. It comes back on a short rest. HP is your actual flesh and blood. Once you're losing HP, you're bleeding, broken, and one bad hit from death.
>
> **Normal hit:** A sword swing deals 8 damage. You have 12 Vitality left. You lose 8 Vitality trying to dodge. No big deal, next short rest, you're fine.
>
> **Same hit, you're out of Vitality:** That 8 damage goes straight to HP. Now you're injured. You need healing magic or a long rest.
>
> **Fireball (AoE):** The wizard drops a fireball that deals 24 damage. Half of that (12) bypasses Vitality and hits HP directly. The other 12 goes to Vitality. So you lose 12 HP and 12 Vit in one shot. This happens because it is unreasonable or impossible to dodge the entire effect from an aoe attack.
>
> **Crit:** An enemy crits you with a dagger. Normally it'd deal 1d4+2 (say 5 damage to Vit). Instead it deals max damage (4+2=6) straight to HP. That one lucky stab just turned a scratch into a real wound.
## Injuries
##### When to check for an Injury
You only ever check when HP is actually harmed (Vitality hits don’t trigger injuries). Check once per source of HP damage (one attack/spell/trap), using the HP portion for mixed cases. Having any injury active will prevent recovery from resting. Once all effects from the injury end you are free to rest again like normal. Some injuries can be cured by resting, in that case the partial resting is applied on this rest but not on any future rests. 
##### Massive Damage Rule
If you take ≥ 50% of your maximum HP in one hit, make a DC 10 Constitution save:
- Fail: you are unconscious until the end of your next turn (or until you take damage again).
- Succeed: you remain standing, but take -1 to all attack rolls on your next turn.
- Make sure that you apply an injury check after seeing if you are unconscious 

> Note: If the target is reduced to 0 HP, they die; do not bother with injuries.

> **How injuries actually work, step by step**
>
> Let's say you have 40 max HP and a giant smashes you for 22 damage to HP (after your Vitality is gone).
>
> **Step 1: Severity:** 22 out of 40 is 55%. That's ≥50%, so it's a Severe injury. It also triggers Massive Damage (≥50% max HP in one hit), so you need to make a DC 10 Con save or fall unconscious until the end of your next turn.
>
> **Step 2: Injury Save:** The DC for Severe is 20. Your Con save is +4. You roll a 14 + 4 = 18 which is a failure. You check the injury mapping table for whatever damage type hit you and apply the condition listed.
>
> **Step 3: Overkill:** If you had passed that save, your Overkill level would go up by 1. Next time you get injured, the DC would be higher or you'd roll with disadvantage. If you keep passing saves, Overkill stacks up to level 5, at which point you just auto-fail your next injury save. 
>
> **If it were a crit:** That same 22-damage hit from a critical would bump the severity up by one level from Severe to Extreme. So even though it's only 55% of your HP, it counts as Extreme (≥75% threshold for severity purposes). The save DC jumps from 20 to 25.
>
> And remember: while you have any injury active, you can't recover from resting properly. You only get half benefits on short and long rests until the injury is resolved.

##### Step 1 Determine Injury Severity (from that one source of HP damage)
Calculate % of max HP lost from that single source (round down any fractional HP when comparing to thresholds):

| Severity      | HP lost from a single source/attack                           |
| ------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Minor         | ≥10% and <25% of max HP                                       |
| Moderate      | ≥25% and <50% of max HP                                       |
| Severe        | ≥50% of max HP (this also triggers Massive Damage separately) |
| Extreme       | ≥75% of max HP (this also triggers Massive Damage separately) |
**Critical Hit upgrade:** When dealing damage with a critical hit the Severity rating will go up by 1 level. So if you only dealt 15% of the targets max health as damage it would still be considered a moderate injury not a minor one like normal. 
##### Step 2 Injury Save (CON)
Make a Constitution saving throw vs the DC for the severity:

| Severity  | Injury Save (CON) DC |
| --------- | -------------------- |
| Minor     | DC 10                |
| Moderate  | DC 15                |
| Severe    | DC 20                |
| Extreme   | DC 25                |
- **Success:** No injury is applied; increase your Overkill level by 1 (to a maximum of 5) (see below).
- **Failure:** apply the conditions listed in the injury mapping table (See [[2.3 Injuries]]).
- **Natural 1:** apply the conditions listed but increase their save DC's by 5 and increase the number of turns they last by 1.
###### Overkill Scaling
Each time you succeed on an Injury Save, your Overkill level increases by 1 (max 5). If you fail an Injury Save, your Overkill level resets to 0. While you have an Overkill level, you suffer the cumulative effects listed below on subsequent Injury Saves until your Overkill level changes. (Effects at higher levels include all effects from lower levels.)

| Overkill Level | Effect on subsequent Injury Saves                  |
| -------------- | -------------------------------------------------- |
| 1              | Roll your next Injury Save with disadvantage.      |
| 2              | +5 to the Injury Save DC (cumulative).             |
| 3              | +10 to the Injury Save DC (cumulative, total +15). |
| 4              | +10 to the Injury Save DC (cumulative, total +25). |
| 5              | You automatically fail your next Injury Save.      |
##### Step 3 Injury Mapping 
(by damage type → apply these Conditions on a failed Injury Save)

For a list of injuries see [[2.3 Injuries]]

> If a minor injury causes a condition, it cannot reduce your HP below 1. When this happens, the condition automatically ends once your HP reaches that point. However, moderate and higher injuries can still be fatal.

For specific details on Conditions use [[2.4 Conditions]]
## Resting
###### Short Rest:  
A short rest is a period of downtime lasting at least 30 minutes, where your character can catch their breath, tend to wounds, etc.

During a short rest, you:
- Regain all your Vitality and 50% of your maximum mana
- Refresh certain class features that refresh on a short rest (they will be specified by the skill)
- You **DO NOT** regain any HP during a short rest
- **If you have an injury:** You only regain 50% of Vitality and Mana.
###### Long Rest:  
A long rest takes 8 hours of uninterrupted rest or sleep. During a long rest, you:
- Fully restore HP, Vitality, and Mana
- Regain expended abilities
- Clear all levels of exhaustion 
- **If you have an injury:** You only regain 50% of your max HP and 75% of Vitality and Mana.

*Optional Rule*: If your Game Master wants to, they may not allow you to take long rests when in a dungeon or exploring a forest because you won’t really be able to get good rest. In this scenario you would only be able to get a long rest when in a house or actual city with a real bed. 

Note: If a long rest is interrupted (e.g., by combat), it must be fully restarted to gain benefits.
### Exhaustion
Exhaustion represents a character's declining condition due to overexertion, starvation, lack of rest, or certain magical effects.

Exhaustion is tracked in levels from 1 to 6, with each level imposing increasingly severe penalties:

| Exhaustion Level | Effect                                         |
| ---------------- | ---------------------------------------------- |
| 1                | Disadvantage on ability checks                 |
| 2                | Speed halved                                   |
| 3                | Disadvantage on attack rolls and saving throws |
| 4                | HP and Vitality max reduced by 50%             |
| 5                | Speed reduced to 5ft                           |
| 6                | Death                                          |

**Note**: These penalties are cumulative. A character with level 3 exhaustion suffers the effects of levels 1, 2, and 3 simultaneously.

> **Yes, the exhaustion levels stack. Here's what that actually means.**
>
> At level 1, you have disadvantage on ability checks, this is annoying but manageable. At level 2, your speed is halved *on top of that*, so now you're bad at skills and slow. At level 3, you also have disadvantage on attacks and saves. Good luck fighting anything. At level 4, your max HP and Vitality are cut in half, so you're both fragile and useless. At level 5, you can move 5 ft per turn, you're basically crawling. At level 6, you die.
>
> You only remove 1 level per long rest. So if you somehow hit level 4 exhaustion, it takes four days of bed rest to fully recover. Spells that remove exhaustion are rare, treat it as a real threat, not a minor debuff.
###### Causes of Exhaustion
- Going without food, water, or sleep for extended periods of time
- Traveling for more than 16 hours without rest
- Failing certain saving throws (e.g., extreme environments or spell/potion effects)
###### Removing Exhaustion
- Long Rest: Removes **1** level of exhaustion.
- Magical Means: Some spells can remove exhaustion instantly.
## Armor Class (AC)
Armor class in Eyum represents how hard it is to be hit. When you make an attack roll against an enemy your roll is compared to their AC (armor class), if you match or exceed the AC then you hit. AC can be gained from many things from such as your Dex Mod, Armor, magic effects and more. Your AC bonus granted from your Dex mod changes depending on several factors. Your Base AC is calculated as 10 + Armor + Dex Bonus. 

| Armor            | Max Dex Mod | If Expert | Dodge Master |
| ---------------- | ----------- | --------- | ------------ |
| **No Armor**     | +4          | N/A       | +7           |
| **Light Armor**  | +5          | +7        | +7           |
| **Medium Armor** | +4          | +6        | +7           |
| **Heavy Armor**  | +3          | +5        | +7           |
The bonus from AC also changes as the number raises. You cannot gain more than +7 AC from your dexterity no matter how high it is. 

| Dex Mod | AC Bonus | \|  | Dex Mod | Ac Bonus |
| ------- | -------- | --- | ------- | -------- |
| +1      | +1       | \|  | +12     | +6       |
| +2      | +2       | \|  | +13     | +6       |
| +3      | +3       | \|  | +14     | +6       |
| +4      | +3       | \|  | +15     | +6       |
| +5      | +4       | \|  | +16     | +6       |
| +6      | +4       | \|  | +17     | +6       |
| +7      | +4       | \|  | => +18  | +7       |
| +8      | +5       | \|  |         |          |
| +9      | +5       | \|  |         |          |
| +10     | +5       | \|  |         |          |
| +11     | +5       | \|  |         |          |
> So if you aren't wearing any armor and have say 20 (+5) in Dex you would have 14 AC, if you have 12 (+2) in Dex you would have 12 AC and so on. For more information on how Armor will effect AC see [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]].

> There is no actual cap on AC, just a cap on how much benefit your Dex can provide for your AC. Things like spells and runes can make your AC much higher than the Dex bonus will ever provide. 
## Affinity

> **How affinities actually work in practice**
>
> Affinity splits into two parts: Generic and Specific.
>
> Generic Affinity is your baseline magical talent. It applies to every spell you cast — it adds to all your spell attack rolls and all your spell save DCs. You can't buy it with Affinity Points (Affp). You only get it from class features, race bonuses, and the occasional feat.
>
> Specific Affinities (Fire, Earth, Water, Air, Radiant, Necrotic, Psychic, and all the advanced ones) are where you spend your Affp. Fire Affinity makes your fire spells hit harder and your fire save DCs higher. More Fire Affinity = more fire damage.
>
> The catch with advanced affinities (Lightning, Magma, Ice/Cold, etc.) is that you need 10+ points in their parent affinities before you can unlock them. Want Lightning spells? You need 10 Fire and 10 Air first. You can't skip straight to the cool stuff.
>
> So a typical caster progression looks like: pump Fire and Air to 10 each → unlock Lightning → now you can spend Affp directly on Lightning. You don't lose the Fire and Air points — they still work. You just opened a new path.
>
> For the full list of what unlocks what, see [[2.1 Affinities]].

## Affinity
Your Affinity represents how attuned you are to a specific element or magical source. Each affinity governs a unique damage type, and monsters will be weak or resistant to different affinities depending on their type. For more information on specific affinities and their unlock conditions, see [[2.1 Affinities]].
#### Generic Affinity
Generic Affinity is your overall magical attunement, your ability to channel energy of any type. Unlike specific affinities, it applies to all spells equally, regardless of element. Generic Affinity cannot be increased with Affinity Points (Affp). Your generic affinity is added to your spell attack rolls and your spell saving throws, for more information see [[6.1 Spells]].
#### Specific Affinities
Specific affinities represent your connection to a particular element: Fire, Earth, Water, Air, Radiant, Necrotic, Psychic, and many advanced hybrid types. Advanced types (Lightning, Steam, Magma, Ice/Cold, etc.) have prerequisites, you must already have 10+ points in their parent affinities. Once unlocked, they can be upgraded like any other affinity. For more information check [[2.1 Affinities]].


========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.4 Actions.md
========================================================================

## Core Actions
Core actions are divided into three main categories: skill checks, combat actions, and social interactions.

- **Skill Checks**: A skill check occurs when a player attempts a specific action, such as climbing a rope. The player rolls a d20 and adds their relevant ability modifier. The GM sets the DC based on the difficulty of the task. If the player meets or exceeds the DC, they succeed.

- **Combat Actions**: Combat is a significant part of the game. On their turn, a player can take the following types of actions:
    - **Action**: A primary action that takes about 6 seconds (the length of a turn). Examples include attacking or casting a spell. Although an action normally takes a full turn, as you level up you will gain multiple action points (Ap) which will allow taking multiple actions in one turn. This applies to Bonus Actions (BAp) and Reactions (Rp) as well. 
    - **Bonus Action**: A secondary action, such as equipping a weapon or using a minor ability.
    - **Reaction**: A response to another entity’s action, such as an opportunity attack or casting a defensive spell. Players can hold an action to trigger a reaction when specific criteria are met.
    - **Free Actions**: Minor actions that require no time, such as speaking or dropping an item.

- **Social Interactions**: Outside of combat, players engage in role-playing conversations with NPCs or each other. Players should act based on their character’s knowledge and motivations, avoiding the use of out-of-character information.
## Turn Economy
Combat is turn-based. Creatures act in initiative order, and your effectiveness depends on how you spend your resources each turn. Understanding and managing your Action Points (Ap), Bonus Action Points (BAp), Reaction Points (Rp), and movement is the core of tactical play in Eyum. 
###### Why action economy matters
Encounters are often won or lost on how many actions each side can meaningfully take. Eyum explicitly points out that action economy is “the single most important factor” in encounter balance, more actions can overwhelm, and locking down a foe’s actions can trivialize them. Design, expect, and respond to that reality. 
###### Companions & Summons
Non-player allies take their own turns on your initiative. By default, a tamed companion (see [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]]) has 1 Ap, 1 BAp, and 0 Rp, and you may spend your Rp to grant it a reaction once per round. (Summoned creatures act per their spell.) 
###### Progression of Ap/BAp/Rp
Your available points can increase with level, as specified in the leveling rules. Check your character\u2019s current totals before planning a turn. At level one you will have 1 Action Point (Ap), 1 Bonus Action Point (BAp) and 1 Reaction Point (Rp). For more information on how you gain these points see [[3.3 Leveling Up]].
## Standard Actions per Turn
On your turn, you may take the following types of actions, depending on how many points you have available:

- **Action (Ap):** Your main combat or ability use. You may take as many actions per turn as you have Action Points (Ap). Common actions (actions all characters can take) include:
    - Attack - Make a melee or ranged weapon attack. Damage is based on weapon and stats.
    - Cast a Spell - Use a known spell, spending Mana and meeting any requirements.
    - Use Item - Use a potion, activate a magic item, etc.
    - Dash - Move an additional distance equal to your Base Movement Speed.
    - Disengage - Avoid provoking opportunity attacks for the rest of your turn.
    - Dodge - Until your next turn, all attacks against you are at disadvantage and you make DEX saves with advantage.
    - Help - Grant an ally within 5 ft advantage on their next ability check or attack.
    - Hide - Make a Stealth check to become hidden. Must be in light cover or darkness.
    - Search - Make a Search check to find traps, hidden creatures, etc.
    - Ready an Action - Hold a specific action until a trigger occurs.
    - Grapple - Attempt to grab a creature within 5 ft. Roll Athletics vs target’s Athletics or Acrobatics. Success: target becomes Grappled (Speed 0 and all attacks made against creature have advantage).
    - Shove - Attempt to push a creature within 5 ft. Roll Athletics vs target’s Athletics or Acrobatics. Success: target is knocked prone and pushed 5 ft.
    - Interact with the Environment - Pull levers, flip switches, shove statues, etc

- **Bonus Action (BAp):** A secondary action, granted by specific class features, spells, or abilities. You may use as many bonus actions as you have Bonus Action Points (BAp) or alternatively you may convert BAp into Ap at a ratio of 2 BAp per 1 Ap, this can not be done in reverse. Examples include (but are not limited to):
    - Activating a subclass/racial/feat ability 
    - Casting a spell labeled as a bonus action
    - Using a class ability like Rage

- **Reaction (Rp):** Used outside of your turn in response to specific triggers (e.g., opportunity attacks, spell triggers, etc.). You may take a number of reactions equal to your Reaction Points (Rp). Examples include (but are not limited to):
    - Opportunity Attack - When a creature leaves your melee range. Make one melee attack.
    - Counterspell - Interrupt a spell being cast.
    - Trigger Abilities - Any ability that uses the phrase "as a reaction."

- **Movement:** You may move up to your Speed each turn. Movement can be split around actions. Dashing will cost an Ap and allow you to move an additional distance equal to your Base Movement Speed, it is possible to take this action multiple times in one turn.
## Holding an Action
**What a “Ready Action” is:**  
On your turn, you may choose to hold an action (or bonus action) and state a specific trigger and the specific action you’ll take when that trigger happens before your next turn.

**How it resolves:**  
When the trigger occurs, your readied action goes off outside your turn (it functions like a reaction), and you carry out the exact action you declared. The readied action still costs the appropriate Action Point (Ap) or Bonus Action Point (BAp). If the trigger doesn’t happen before your next turn, the action is lost.
#### Step-by-step
1. **Declare you’re readying.**  
    “I ready an action.” (This happens on **your** turn.)
2. **Specify the trigger and the action.**  
    Give both parts clearly (trigger -> response), e.g., “If the ogre opens the door, I fire my bow.” You must explicitly name the trigger and the action.
3. **Pay the cost.**  
    A readied action costs Ap; a readied bonus action costs BAp. (You are holding one of the standard actions you could normally take.)
4. **Wait for the trigger.**  
    If it happens before your next turn, your readied action resolves off-turn like a reaction; otherwise, it expires unused.
#### What can you ready?
You may ready any specific action or bonus action you could normally take when it’s your turn (for example, from the “Common actions” list): Attack, Cast a Spell, Use Item, Dash, or Interact with the Environment, as long as you name exactly which one you’re holding.
#### Examples 
- “I wait until the goblin enters the room, then I fire my crossbow.” 
- “If the door opens, I fire my bow.” 
- “If the sentry steps into the hall, I Dash across to the cover.” 
    - This allows you to move when it is not your turn, but you must move to a specific location that you specify on your turn.
    - Moving past an enemy like this will always provoke Opportunity attacks, even if the Disengage Action was taken on your turn. 
- “If our ally hauls the lever, I Use Item to toss the rope down.” 

> In every case, you’ll resolve the readied option using the normal rules for that action (e.g., make an Attack roll when you ready Attack). 
#### Timing & conflicts (what happens “at the same time”?)
If your readied action interrupts something at the exact same moment as another interrupt (for example, two characters’ responses would both trigger together), use the Handbook’s Interrupting Actions guidance: resolve simultaneous interrupts in initiative order. 
#### Quick table template
- Trigger: "If/when {clear event}"
- Action: "I {specific action or bonus action}."
- Cost: "This uses Ap/BAp."
- Expiry: "If it doesn't happen before my next turn, it's lost."

> The readied action must have a specific trigger, it cannot be something generic like I do this if anything bad happens, it needs to be something specific which your character would be able to see and or react to. 

> You cannot dash as a readied action as it uses movement does not consume Ap or BAp (technically speaking you could dash as your readied action but nothing would happen).
## Interrupting Actions
**What an interrupt is.**  
Some abilities and spells explicitly say they **interrupt another creature’s action**, classic examples are spells like **Counterspell**. When the listed trigger occurs, you may use the specified ability **outside your turn** to interject before the original action completes.

**How it’s paid for.**  
Use the **cost specified** by the effect. If the interrupt is a **reaction** (as it usually is), it consumes **Reaction Points (Rp)**; If the interrupt is a **spell**, it also costs its **Mana** as listed in the spell.

**When it happens in time.**  
An eligible interrupt **resolves immediately when its trigger fires**, before the triggering action finishes. If the interrupt **cancels or overrides** the action (as the effect’s text allows), the original action does not complete. Otherwise, the original action proceeds after the interrupt resolves.

**If two (or more) interrupts collide.**  
When two characters try to interrupt **at the same moment**, resolve them **in initiative order** (highest acting first). After resolving the earlier interrupt, re-check whether the later one still applies.
## Turn Order and Initiative
**What this covers.** 
Combat in The World of Eyum is resolved in **turns**. **Initiative** determines who acts first and in what sequence the rest of the combatants take their turns. You’ll roll once at the start of a fight, and that order governs the flow of play.
#### What initiative is (and why it matters)
Initiative is a simple ranking of all combatants from fastest to slowest for the encounter. High initiative lets you act sooner, move, attack, cast, before your opponents can respond. The GM records the results so everyone knows “who goes when,” and then the table proceeds in that order each round.
#### How initiative is calculated
At the start of combat, **each character rolls 1d20** and adds their **initiative bonus**:  
**Dexterity modifier + any bonuses from race, feats, class abilities, or items.**

**Ties:** If two or more characters roll the same number, the one with the **higher Dexterity score** goes first. If still tied, those characters **reroll** to determine who goes first.
#### Ambushes and the Surprise Round
If one side successfully **ambushes** the other, the **ambushing party always goes first**, ignoring normal initiative order for that initial exchange. Resolve a **surprise round** before regular initiative begins.
- During the **surprise round**, **all members of the ambushing party** take their turns **before** the actual initiative of the fight. If the ambushers then also win normal initiative, they can effectively take **two turns** before the unaware side acts (surprise round → first regular round).
- **Only creatures unaware** of the ambush are affected. If a creature is **immune to surprise** or **aware** of the ambush, it **acts during the surprise round like normal**.

After the surprise round, proceed into the standard initiative order for the rest of combat.

> **How surprise rounds actually work (and why they're so deadly)**
>
> Say your party ambushes a group of bandits. Your party all pass their Stealth checks; the bandits fail their spot. That's a successful ambush.
>
> **Surprise round:** The entire party takes their turns first (or anyone who passed their checks). The bandits cannot take actions this turn.
>
> **Then initiative gets rolled normally.** If the party also wins initiative, they get another full round of actions before the bandits ever move. That's two full rounds for the party before the enemy does anything. A well-executed ambush can end a fight before it starts.

#### Delaying your turn
A character may **delay their turn**, moving themselves **to a lower position** in the initiative order **for the rest of the combat**. This must be declared **before taking any actions**. Once you choose to delay, your **new initiative position is permanent** for that encounter.
#### Organizing turns at the table
To keep combat readable, we recommend one of two ways to structure the sequence once initiative is set:
- **Grouped Initiative:** All **players act together**, then all **enemies act** (or vice versa). Use this for **speed and simplicity**, fewer hand-offs, quicker rounds.
- **Individual Initiative:** Every combatant acts strictly in **rolled order**. Use this for **tactical precision** and **dramatic tension**, this will take longer but is more realistic.
Both are valid; choose the one that fits your table’s pacing goals.
#### Quick flow reference (combat start to first round)
1. **Roll initiative** and establish the order. The GM records it.
2. If there’s a **successful ambush**, run the **surprise round** first (only against unaware targets as noted).
3. Proceed through **turns** using either **grouped** or **individual** initiative, per your table’s choice.
4. On your turn, take actions per the **turn economy** (Ap/BAp/Movement; Rp when triggered outside your turn).



========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.5 Mounts and Familiars.md
========================================================================

## Mounting Rules
Mounting allows characters to ride creatures into battle, across dangerous terrain, or through long-distance travel. A mount must be at least 1 Size larger than its rider unless otherwise stated.

Mounts are separated into three broad categories:

- **Domestic Mounts**: Horses, camels, elk, etc.
