- **War Mounts**: Creatures trained for battle.
- **Exotic Mounts**: Magical, monstrous, or rare creatures.

Most mounts will require a saddle, see [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]] for more. 
Creatures with Intelligence 6+ may refuse riders they dislike (at GM's digression, may be tamed with Animal Handling Check).

**Mounting and Dismounting**
- Mounting or dismounting costs:
    - 1 Bonus Action Point
    - OR half your movement (your choice).
- You must be adjacent to the mount.
- The mount must be willing, restrained, or controlled.

If you are knocked prone while mounted, you immediately dismount into an adjacent space.
###### Mounted Movement and Initiative 
- The rider and mount share initiative.
- The mount moves during the rider’s turn.
- When moving into danger (Any situation which could cause harm to the mount counts as danger, this will be chosen at the Gm's digression): 
	- Directing a war/exotic mount requires no action.
	- Directing an domestic mount into danger requires an Animal Handling check (DC 12–20).
	- Failure causes the mount to flee away from the source of danger.
###### Mounted Combat
While mounted:
- You occupy the mount’s space.
- Melee attacks gain Advantage if:
    - using a weapon with +5 ft Reach
    - OR the mount is at least 2 Sizes larger than the target.
- Creatures smaller than your mount have Disadvantage attacking you in melee unless they can reach you.
- Ranged attacks made while the mount moved this turn are made with Disadvantage unless you possess a specific feat (see [[3.3 Leveling Up]]).
- Charging:
	- If a mount moves at least 30 ft directly toward a creature the rider may make one mounted charge attack:
		- On hit target must succeed a Strength Save DC = 8 + rider Proficiency + mount Strength modifier or be knocked Prone.
		- Some mounts grant additional charge effects.
###### Falling From a Mount
If the mount dies, falls prone or is forcibly moved the rider must succeed a DC 10 Dexterity Save or fall prone in an adjacent space and take 1d6 Bludgeoning damage.
If falling from a flying mount resolve normal falling damage (see [[1.6 Other Rules]]).
###### Taking Care of Your Mount
Mounts will need to eat, drink, and sleep. How much food, water, and sleep they need will be up to your GM. 
###### Mounts
| Mount              | Speed | Size   | Vitality | Health | AC  | Type     | Price      | Traits                                                            |
| ------------------ | ----- | ------ | -------- | ------ | --- | -------- | ---------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Horse              | 60 ft | Large  | 20       | 10     | 12  | Domestic | 2 Gold     | Advantage on long-distance travel checks.                         |
| Pony               | 40 ft | Medium | 15       | 8      | 10  | Domestic | 1 Gold     | None                                                              |
| Camel              | 50 ft | Large  | 25       | 15     | 11  | Domestic | 1 Gold     | Can survive days without water.                                   |
| Elk                | 70 ft | Large  | 20       | 10     | 12  | Domestic | 3 Gold     | None                                                              |
| Riding Ox          | 30 ft | Large  | 50       | 30     | 13  | Domestic | 1 Gold     | Advantage resisting forced movement and immune to prone.          |
| Giant Boar         | 40 ft | Large  | 40       | 25     | 13  | Exotic   | 1 Gold     | Charge attacks deal +1d8 damage. Animal Handling DC raised by +3. |
| Giant Ram          | 50 ft | Large  | 35       | 20     | 12  | Exotic   | 2 Gold     | Charge attack prone DC raised by +5.                              |
| Giant Lizard       | 40 ft | Large  | 25       | 15     | 15  | Exotic   | 2 Gold     | Climb speed 80 ft. Can cling to walls and ceilings.               |
| Marsh Lizard       | 50 ft | Large  | 30       | 20     | 15  | Exotic   | 2 Gold     | Swim speed 60 ft.                                                 |
| Shellback Tortoise | 20 ft | Huge   | 80       | 60     | 20  | Exotic   | 3 Gold     | Riders gain Half Cover while mounted.                             |
| River Serpent      | 60 ft | Huge   | 50       | 35     | 10  | Exotic   | 2 Gold     | Swim speed 100 ft. Can carry two riders.                          |
| Wyvern             | 80 ft | Huge   | 100      | 50     | 15  | Flying   | 8 Platinum | Fly 120 ft.                                                       |
| Warhorse           | 60 ft | Large  | 35       | 20     | 17  | War      | 5 Platinum | Immune to frightened condition, will never flee from danger.      |
| Basilisk Hound     | 40 ft | Large  | 40       | 30     | 16  | War      | 1 Platinum | Frightens other mounts passively (DC 12 Wis Save)                 |
| War Elephant       | 40 ft | Huge   | 100      | 70     | 19  | War      | 7 Platinum | May carry up to 4 riders.                                         |
## Familiars
A familiar is a monster you have tamed and bonded with, turning it into a loyal companion that fights alongside you, assists outside of combat, and grows in power as you do. Any monster with a statblock (see [[7.1 Monsters]]) can potentially become a familiar, provided you have the means to tame it.

Familiars are distinct from mounts, a familiar need not be rideable, though a large enough familiar may serve as both. They are also distinct from summons, which are temporary magical constructs. A familiar is a permanent, living creature that shares your journey.
###### Prerequisites
To tame and command a familiar, you must first unlock the ability to do so through the **Monster Specialist** path (see [[3.3 Leveling Up]]):

| Monster Type | Required Level                    | Taming Unlock |
| ------------ | --------------------------------- | ------------- |
| Slimes       | Monster Specialist: Slime Rancher | Level 3.5     |
| Golems       | Monster Specialist: Golem Tamer   | Level 3.5     |
| Dragons      | Monster Specialist: Dragonslayer  | Level 5.6     |

> *Unless otherwise specified by a ability or feat, you may only have one active familiar at a time. You may dismiss a familiar to a safe location (GM's discretion) and recall it later with a short rest.*
###### Taming a Monster
To tame a wild monster, you must meet its taming prerequisites and succeed on a contested check:

1. The monster must be at or below the maximum level you can tame (see Familiar Maximum Level below).
2. You must make an Animal Handling check contested by the monster's Wisdom Save.
   - **DC modifier**: The monster's Affinity modifier is added to the monster's roll.
   - If the monster is already Hostile toward you, the check is made with Disadvantage and suffers a -5 penalty.
   - If the monster is Restrained, Paralyzed, or Unconscious, the check has Advantage.
1. Taming takes 10 minutes of uninterrupted focus. If you take damage during this time, you must succeed a DC 10 Constitution Save or the attempt fails and the monster becomes hostile.
2. On a success, the monster becomes docile and bonded to you as your familiar. On a failure, you may try again after 24 hours.
###### Familiar Maximum Level
A familiar's maximum level is capped based on its species and your current level. A familiar cannot exceed this cap regardless of experience gained.

| Monster Type      | Max Familiar Level            |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Slimes            | 1/2 your level (rounded down) |
| Golems            | 1/3 your level (rounded down) |
| Dragons           | 1/5 your level (rounded down) |
| Other/Unspecified | 1/2 your level (rounded down) |

> *Example: A level 10 Player (with the Slime Rancher Level 3.5 Unlock) can tame slimes up to level 5. A level 20 Player (with the Dragonslayer level 5.6 Unlock) can tame dragons up to level 4.*

You **CANNOT** tame monsters who are above your Max familiar cap by any means. 
###### Familiar Basics
- A familiar uses its own monster statblock (see the appropriate monster list in [[7.1 Monsters]]]), including its stats, Vitality, Health, Mana, AC, Speed, damage, passives, and actives, exactly as written.
- The familiar shares your initiative and acts on your turn.
- By default, a familiar has 1 Ap, 1 BAp, and 0 Rp per turn (per the standard companion rules in [[1.4 Actions]]).
- You may spend your own Reaction Points (Rp) to grant your familiar a reaction once per round.
- A familiar is a creature in its own right, it occupies space, provokes opportunity attacks, can be targeted, and dies when it reaches 0 HP.
- You may communicate basic commands to your familiar as a Free Action on your turn. Complex tactical instructions require the familiar to have Intelligence 6+.
###### Familiar Leveling
A familiar gains experience alongside the party. Each time you level up and your familiar is not yet at it's maximum level, it gains a level.

Each time a familiar gains a level, apply the standard monster level-up bonuses from [[7.1 Monsters]]:

| Per Level | Bonus |
|---|---|
| Vitality | +1 Hit Die (roll or average) |
| Health | +1 Hit Die (roll or average) |
| Mana | +1 Hit Die (roll or average) |
| Generic Damage Mod | +1 |
| Affinity | +1 to its primary Affinity |
| AC | +1 |
| Stats (STR, CON, DEX) | +1 to each |
| Stats (WIS, INT, CHA) | +1 to each every 3 levels (3, 6, 9, etc.) |

> This is identical to the monster leveling rules in [[7.1 Monsters]]. The familiar's statblock at its current level can be built by applying these bonuses to its level 1 base.

Some Monster Specialist path upgrades (e.g., Golem Tamer's overcharge) may provide alternative methods of leveling a familiar, those override these standard rules where they apply but can never exceed the monsters maximum level.
###### Familiar Bond
Over time, your familiar grows more attuned to you. After the familiar has served you for at least 3 levels gained while bonded, it forms a Bond with you:

- While your familiar is within 30 ft of you, you and the familiar gain +1 to all Saving Throws.
- Your familiar gains its own Reaction Point (Rp) and no longer needs you to sacrifice yours. 
- The familiar gains +2 to its highest stat.

This bond is broken if the familiar dies or if you dismiss it permanently. A new familiar must earn its own bond from scratch.
###### Familiar Equipment
A familiar may wear equipment appropriate to its body type, chosen from the Mounts/Familiar Equipment table in [[2.5 Weapons, Armors, and Tools]]. Armor functions identically for familiars as they do for mounts.

A familiar may also carry items, pouches, or packs at the GM's discretion, based on its size and strength.
###### Multiple Familiars
Unless an ability explicitly states otherwise, you may only have one active familiar at a time. You may maintain a bond with only one familiar at a time, switching to a new familiar breaks the previous bond.

Some Monster Specialist abilities (e.g., Golem Tamer's multiple commands) may allow you to control multiple creatures, but only one of them counts as your bonded familiar for the purposes of the Familiar Bond feature and the standard companion action rules.
###### Losing a Familiar
If your familiar reaches 0 HP, it is dying and follows the same death and dying rules as a player character (see [[1.3 Player Stats]]). If it dies, the bond is broken.
- A dead familiar can be revived with magic (e.g., *Revivify*, *Raise Dead*) just as any creature can up to a point. 
- If a familiar is permanently lost, you may tame a new one. 

If you release a familiar willingly, it returns to the wild with whatever equipment it was wearing (unless you retrieve it before release). It retains its memories and will not be hostile toward you unless provoked but loses it's bond, if you re-tame the same creature to be a familiar it only takes one level to restore its bond.
###### Familiars and Mounts
A familiar that is at least one Size larger than you (see its statblock entry for Size) may serve as a mount, following the mounted combat rules at the top of this section.  If a familiar is acting as a mount, it uses its own Ap, BAp, and Rp for movement and actions during the mount phase of your turn, separate from your own action economy (as per standard mounted combat rules).


========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.6 Other Rules.md
========================================================================

## Light
Characters interact with the world based on what they can see, and in Eyum, not everyone sees the same way. Some races thrive in pitch darkness; others need light to survive underground. This section explains how light and vision work in the game world.

#### Light Levels

| Light Level  | Effect                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Bright Light | Full visibility. Daylight, torches within 20 ft, glowing crystals, etc. No penalties.                                                                                                               |
| Dim Light    | Partial visibility. Shadows, twilight, or the edge of torchlight (20-40 ft). Creatures may gain concealment (+3 to AC) unless you have Low-Light or Darkvision. Disadvantage on Search/Spot Checks. |
| Darkness     | No natural visibility. Caves, night with no moon, deep dungeons. You cannot see without Darkvision, magic, or a light source. Double disadvantage on attack rolls and Search/Spot Checks            |
#### Types of Vision

| Type             | Range           | Description                                                                                                                                                       |
| ---------------- | --------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Normal Vision    | N/A             | You see clearly in bright light and dimly in dim light. You cannot see in darkness without a light source.                                                        |
| Low-Light Vision | N/A             | You treat dim light as bright light for visibility. You still cannot see in complete darkness.                                                                    |
| Darkvision       | 60 ft (default) | You can see in total darkness as if it were dim light, but only in shades of gray. You cannot discern color.                                                      |
| True Sight       | Varies          | You see everything as it truly is: invisible creatures, hidden illusions, ethereal forms, and magical darkness. Only obtained through magic or special abilities. |
#### Light Sources

| Source        | Bright Light     | Dim Light |
| ------------- | ---------------- | --------- |
| Torch         | 20 ft            | 20 ft     |
| Lantern       | 30 ft            | 30 ft     |
| Light (spell) | 30 ft            | 30 ft     |
| Campfire      | 40 ft            | 40 ft     |
#### Notes:
- Torches burn for 1 hour. You can carry one in a free hand.
- Lanterns require oil and must be lit, they last for 5 hours.
#### Darkvision Rules
- Most underground, shadow-aligned, or nocturnal races have Darkvision (60 ft) by default. A few powerful creatures may have 120 ft.
- Darkvision does not pierce magical darkness. It also renders everything in gray-scale, making color identification impossible.
- If you have Darkvision, you ignore darkness penalties within range.
- If you do not have it, you are effectively blind in total darkness.
#### Other Rules
- Stealth in Dim Light: Characters in dim light may gain +2 to Stealth checks if their opponents lack Low-Light or Darkvision.
- Darkness as Terrain: Unless the creature has a means to see Total Darkness always counts as Difficult Terrain
## Movement, Terrain, and Cover
###### Movement Basics
On your turn, you can move up to your Speed in feet. Movement can be split around actions (e.g., move 15 ft, attack, then move another 15 ft). You may move through allies' spaces, but not through enemy spaces unless a rule/spell allows it (More detailed blow). Typically speaking a single square on your game board will be 5 ft but the GM may change the scale as they wish.  
###### Climbing
- Climbing costs double movement unless stated otherwise.
- Example: Climbing 10 feet uses 20 feet of movement.
- If a surface is especially slick or sheer, the GM may require an Athletics check to climb successfully.
- Characters with climbing gear, claws, or spider-like limbs may ignore or reduce movement penalties.
###### Swimming
- Swimming also costs double movement unless the creature has a swim speed.
- Characters may be required to make an Athletics check in rough waters, strong currents, or while swimming in armor.
- Heavy armor imposes disadvantage on swim checks unless you have a swim speed.
###### Flying
- Creatures with a fly speed use it normally while airborne.
- Flying creatures must remain in motion unless hovering (specified by the thing that gives you flight). If they are knocked prone or restrained, they usually fall.
- The GM may call for checks (e.g., DEX saves) to avoid crashing in bad weather or tight spaces.
###### Gliding
- Creatures with a glide speed may glide in a straight line to safely and slowly fall for a distance. 

 > Note: Characters cannot fly without magic, a spell, a mount (see [[1.5 Mounts and Familiars]]), or a natural fly speed.
 
 There are caps on movement speed based on the type of movement. Any trait, action, feature, etc which would increase movement speed beyond the set cap will do nothing instead. 

| Speed Type | Speed Cap |
| ---------- | --------- |
| Regular    | 300       |
| Swimming   | 250       |
| Flying     | 500       |
###### Difficult Terrain
Difficult terrain represents cluttered, hazardous, or unstable ground, such as mud, rubble, deep snow, or dense forest.
- Moving through difficult terrain costs double movement.
- Example: 10 ft of difficult terrain uses 20 ft of movement.
- Some abilities or spells allow you to ignore difficult terrain.

Note: Difficult terrain does not stack, you either treat a space as difficult or not.

> **Movement, cover, and opportunity attacks all at once here's how they interact**
>
> Say you're fighting on a staircase. An archer has high ground (15 ft up) behind a battlement, and an enemy is between you and the stairs.
>
> **Moving past the enemy:** You try to run up the stairs. As soon as you leave the enemies reach, they get an opportunity attack, one melee swing, using their reaction. If you took the Disengage action first, you'd avoid that. If they land the hit your movement is stopped. 
>
> **Difficult terrain:** Halfway up, there's rubble (difficult terrain). Moving 10 ft through it costs 20 ft of movement. Plan accordingly you might not make it all the way up in one turn.
>
> **Cover from the archer:** The archer has 3/4 cover from the battlement (+5 AC, advantage on Dex saves). You shoot back, but your effective AC against them is 5 higher. Unless you get around the cover, you're at a big disadvantage.
>
> **High ground:** The archer is 15 ft above you. That means they have advantage on attacks against you, and you have disadvantage on attacks against them. Combined with their cover bonus, they're very hard to hit from below.

###### Cover
Cover offers protection from ranged attacks, spells, or other effects that require line of sight. A creature behind cover is harder to hit.

There are three types of cover:

| Type of Cover  | Bonus                               |
| -------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Half Cover     | +2 to AC and Advantage on DEX saves |
| Three-Quarters | +5 to AC and Advantage DEX saves    |
| Total Cover    | Cannot be targeted directly         |
###### High Ground
If a creature has **high ground** (is at least 1 foot above the head of another creature) it will have advantage on attack rolls against creatures lower than it and creatures beneath it will have disadvantages on attacks made against them. This can by bypassed with specific skills and feats but applies to all attacks made within range. 
###### Flanking
When you attack a creature from behind while an ally is engaging it from the front, you gain advantage on your attack roll. Additionally, the target has disadvantage on saving throws against your effects during this time.
###### Opportunity Attacks
Moving through an enemy’s reach provokes an opportunity attack. When this occurs, the enemy may use their Reaction (**See [[1.4 Actions]]**) to make a melee attack against you as you pass. This rule applies to all creatures unless a feat, spell, or ability states otherwise. In order to do an opportunity attack you must be wielding a melee weapon. This attack interrupts their movement but does not stop it unless a separate effect applies such as knocking them prone.

For example:
- If a monster moves past you, you may make an opportunity attack and vice versa.

However, If you move past a monster while invisible or after taking the Disengage action, you do not provoke opportunity attacks.
###### Fall Damage
For every 5 ft you fall you take 1d4 bludgeoning damage. 
If you fall more than 10 ft you are automatically knocked prone. 
For every 5 ft past 25 ft you fall you gain 1 stack of hurting, this hurting will last for 5 rounds instead of 2. 
Damage after 50 ft increases from 1d4 per 5 ft to 1d8 per 5 ft (for the entire fall distance). 
## Sizes
###### General Size Rules:
- Reach:  
    Typically, a creature’s reach is the same as their occupied space unless otherwise noted.
- Movement Through Spaces:  
    You may move through spaces occupied by creatures at least two size categories larger than you without penalty. Smaller size differences require contested checks or special circumstances (GM discretion).
- Grappling & Shoving:  
    You cannot grapple or shove creatures more than one size category larger than yourself without special abilities or magic, however grappling or shoving a creature two or more sizes down grants advantage on the grapple or shove roll (See [[1.4 Actions]]).

| Size Category  | Space Occupied | Squares Occupied | Gameplay Effects                                                                                                                                                                       |
| -------------- | -------------- | ---------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Tiny           | <2.5 ft        | 1                | Advantage on stealth checks, can move through occupied spaces of Medium or larger creatures with no penalty, disadvantage on strength-based checks against Medium or larger creatures. |
| Small          | 2.5 - 4.9 ft   | 1                | Advantage on stealth checks in appropriate environments.                                                                                                                               |
| Medium         | 5 - 9 ft       | 1                | Standard creature size with no special modifiers.                                                                                                                                      |
| Large          | 10 - 14 ft     | 2                | Reach increases by +5 feet, disadvantage on stealth checks.                                                                                                                            |
| Huge           | 15 - 19 ft     | 3                | Reach increases by +10 feet, double disadvantage on stealth checks, double advantage on rolls against being moved or grappled by Small or smaller creatures.                           |
| Gargantuan     | >19 ft         | 4                | Reach increases by +15 feet, triple disadvantage on stealth checks, double advantage on rolls against being moved or grappled by Medium or smaller creatures.                          |
## Ranged Attack Distances
Ranged attacks in The World of Eyum have structured distance brackets that determine how difficult they are to make. Each ranged weapon or spell lists a base range, which defines how far it can be used normally. Attacks beyond that range are possible but carry penalties.

There are three range brackets:
Normal Range:
- Any attack made within the weapon’s or spell’s base range is considered to be at normal range.
- These attacks are resolved as usual, with no penalties.

Long Range:
- Long range is double the weapon’s base range (2×).
- Attacks made at long range are made at disadvantage unless the weapon, spell, or class feature negates this.
- Some weapons (such as bows and thrown weapons) explicitly list a second range value to mark this upper limit.  
    - Example: 30/90 means 30 ft normal, up to 90 ft at disadvantage.

Extreme Range (Optional Rule):
- This optional bracket allows characters to shoot up to triple the base range (3×), but attacks are made at double disadvantage (roll 3 dice, take the lowest).
- This can only be attempted with weapons that logically support high arc or exceptional distance (e.g., longbows, ballistae).
- This rule is not used by default and must be approved by the GM on a case-by-case basis.

Other Rules for Ranged Attacks:
- You cannot target creatures beyond your maximum effective range (either long or extreme, depending on what’s allowed).
- Line of sight and cover still apply at all ranges.
- Some magical spells or features may override these limitations or allow auto-hits, tracking, or guided projectiles.
## Stacking Effects
**Note:** This is for spell effects not for conditions, conditions have their own rules. 

If multiple effects grant the same type of bonus (for example, two spells that both increase AC), only the highest bonus applies unless an effect specifically states it can stack.

Bonuses from different types of sources (such as a spell and a rune) can stack, as long as neither effect explicitly overrides the other.

In practice, this means that natural armor and worn armor do not stack, two different AC-boosting spells do not stack, but a potion that raises AC and worn armor do stack.

> **What stacks and what doesn't, in plain terms**
>
> The golden rule: same source, no stack. Different sources, yes stack.
>
> **Does not stack (same type):**
> - Two different spells that both boost AC only the highest applies.
> - Natural armor (armor granted from racial bonuses) + worn armor as they cover the same thing. 
>
> **Does stack (different types):**
> - A spell that boosts AC + a magic item that boosts AC + a rune that boosts AC, all different sources, all stack.
> - A potion that gives +2 AC + your armor, potion is a temporary effect, armor is worn equipment. They stack.
>
> When in doubt, ask: are these coming from the same kind of thing? If yes, only the best one works. If no, they add up.

## Underwater Combat
Combat underwater functions differently due to resistance, visibility, and breathing limits. The following rules apply while fighting in submerged environments:

Movement:
- Creatures without a swim speed move at half their standard speed underwater.
- Wearing medium or heavy armor imposes disadvantage on swim checks.
- Dash and Disengage actions work normally, but terrain is always treated as difficult unless you have a swim speed.

Breathing:
- Characters without water-breathing abilities can hold their breath for a number of rounds equal to 1 + their Constitution modifier (minimum 1).
- After that, they begin suffocating (see [[2.4 Conditions]] → Suffocating).

Attacks:
- Melee weapon attacks are made at disadvantage unless the weapon is:
- A dagger, spear, trident, or shortsword
- Or otherwise specifically adapted for underwater use (at GM discretion)
- Ranged weapon attacks automatically miss beyond their first range increment, and are made at disadvantage even within that range unless the weapon is thrown (e.g., javelin or dagger).
- Spells with verbal components cannot be cast unless the caster can breathe underwater or uses magic to bypass the requirement.
- Area-of-effect spells behave normally unless otherwise limited by the environment (e.g., fire-based spells may not work or deal reduced damage, this will happen at the GM’s discretion).

Visibility:
- Underwater vision is limited to 60 ft in clear water, or 30 ft in murky water.
- Darkvision works normally in water but does not improve clarity.
- Light-based spells only illuminate half their normal radius underwater unless specified otherwise.
## Inspiration
Inspiration is a special reward the GM can grant players for exceptional moments of roleplay, clever problem-solving, emotional depth, or creative actions that enhance the game.
The GM may award 1 point of Inspiration when a player:
- Deeply role plays their character’s beliefs, flaws, or background.
- Solves a puzzle or problem in an especially creative way.
- Enhances the group’s enjoyment through immersion or storytelling.
- Impresses the table with a cool, cinematic moment.

There is no limit to how many times you can earn Inspiration, it is tracked as a tally, not a binary on/off switch. You can hold multiple points at once unless the GM sets a limit.
Inspiration may be spent to gain advantage on any d20 roll, standard advantage rules apply. 
You must declare that you are using Inspiration before the result of the roll is revealed, but you do not have to declare it before you roll.
Once spent, the point(s) are consumed.
## Going Past the Limits With Conversions
At extreme levels, characters may exceed built-in caps or gain features they no longer need. Unless stated otherwise, excess gains are lost with no compensation. Certain cases grant a "pity" conversion, listed below.
#### Learning Spells
If you already know every spell you wish to learn, or do not meet the prerequisites for any remaining spells, you may instead gain +1 Affinity Point. You may not hold this spell gain for later; you must choose the Affinity Point immediately.
#### Proficiency and Expertise Caps
If you gain proficiency in a skill you are already proficient in, or expertise in a skill you already have expertise in you may instead gain 3 Skill Points per proficiency or 5 Skill Points per expertise in that skill. If you gain a Proficiency or Expertise but already have Proficiency and Expertise in everything you may instead convert it to 3 Skill Points. 
#### Skill Points
Skills have a maximum modifier of 20. Once all your skills are at +20, any further Skill Points you gain (from leveling, paths, or feats) may be converted into 1 Stat Point per 3 Skill Points. You may make this conversion at any time after all skills are at 20. If you want to convert Skill Points into stats before reaching the Maximum 20 in all Skills then you may convert them at a ratio of 1 Stat Point per 10 Skill Points instead. 
#### Feats
There are a limited number of unique feats. Once you have all the feats you want, or only repeatable feats remain (such as Skill Expert), you may convert a feat gain into 2 Stat Points. You must choose the conversion immediately; you may not hold the feat for later.


========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/1.0 The basics/1.7 Actually Playing The Game.md
========================================================================

> **Read this first:** This section is meant to be read after you’ve created your character and gone through most of the core rules. If your GM asked you to start here instead, that’s fine, just know this section assumes you already understand basics like dice rolls, stats, and actions. If anything here doesn’t make sense, start at the beginning or ask your GM for help.

> If you still need to create your character then see [[3.1 Building Your Character]].

**What this section does:** It shows you what actually happens at the table, from setup to moment-to-moment play, to how combat is structured, to what a “typical session” feels like. New to TTRpGs? You’re in the right place
## What You Need to Play
You can run Eyum with physical pieces on a grid **or** purely in your imagination. Both are valid, pick the one that fits your group.
#### Option 1: Using Miniatures (Recommended)
You’ll need:
- A **miniature or token for each character** (painted model, LEGO, coin, paper square, anything clearly identifiable).
- **Extra tokens** for monsters/NPCs (GM’s side).
- A **grid board or mat**; each square usually represents **5 feet**, great for measuring distance and ranges.
- **Terrain markers** (paper/blocks/colors) to show walls, pits, rivers, etc. Example: blue = water, black = pit, gray = wall.

**Why use minis?** Clear distances and obstacles make tactical choices easier and faster.
#### Option 2: Theater of the Mind (No Miniatures)
No board required. The GM describes the scene and you visualize it:
- GM example: “You’re in a crumbling temple; a large statue ahead, a hallway left.”
- Player example: “I walk to the statue and examine it.”

**Distances stay abstract** unless the GM decides otherwise (often in combat).  
**Trade-off:** Easy to set up and great online, but complex combats are harder to track without a grid (screen-share maps can help).
#### Bring these to each session:
- **Character sheet** (printed or digital)
- **Dice set** (or app)
- **Pencil/notes**
- **Mini or token** (if using minis)
## What Happens During Play
Eyum proceeds as a conversation, punctuated by rolls **only when outcomes are uncertain**.
#### 1) The GM Describes a Scene
They open with what you see and hear (peaceful town, dark forest, trapped corridor, etc.). Example: “A dim tavern, air thick with ale and smoke; a hooded figure sits alone”.
#### 2) Players React
You say what your character attempts: talk to an NPC, search, investigate, prepare for trouble, ask clarifying questions.  
Play in-character: bold? Approach the hooded figure. Cautious? Watch from shadows.
#### 3) The GM Resolves Actions
- **Trivial actions** (“I cross the room”) just happen.
- **Risky/uncertain actions** call for a roll (usually d20): add relevant modifiers; compare to a **DC** the GM uses to judge difficulty.  
    _Example:_ “I sneak past the guards” → “Roll **Stealth**” → GM compares your total vs. the guards’ passive perception, then narrates success or failure.
## Combat - Structured, Tactical Action
When blades come out (or spells fly), Eyum switches to a turn structure so everyone gets a clear chance to act.
#### Step 1: Roll Initiative
Everyone rolls 1d20 + initiative bonus (Dex-based) to set turn order. The GM tracks who goes when.
#### Step 2: Your Turn
Each turn you act using your Action Points (Ap), Bonus Action Points (BAp), and Reaction Points (Rp). Most characters start with 1 of each On your turn, you can:
- **Move** up to your Speed (e.g., 30 ft = 6 squares)
- **Take an Action** (attack, cast a spell, dash, etc.)
- **Take a Bonus Action** (if available)
- **Use Free Actions** (talk, drop items, simple interactions)

> For more information on actions, bonus actions, and reactions see [[1.4 Actions]].

Range/position come from the grid or the GM’s description; you can attack, cast, dodge, help, hide, or interact with the environment.
#### Step 3: Reactions and Interrupts
Even outside your turn, you may act if triggered (e.g., opportunity attacks), but only under those specific conditions.
#### Step 4: GM Controls Enemies
The GM runs monsters with their own stats/abilities using the same core rules.
##### Quick Example (Wolves)
- GM: “Three wolves snarl from the treeline, **roll initiative**.”
- You: “I roll **14**.”
- On your turn: move 10 ft, attack with your axe (roll d20 + STR + attack bonus), roll damage, end turn.
- GM describes the wolves’ response; next player goes.
## Between Combat - Exploration and Roleplay
Most of the game is not combat. You’ll explore towns, investigate ruins, talk to NPCs, solve puzzles, and make decisions that shape the story.
You can:
- Buy and sell items
- Take on quests
- Rest and recover
- Roleplay your character’s beliefs, flaws, and motivations
- Interact with other players and NPCs
- Track time and resources (if your GM includes survival elements)

## Table Etiquette
#### Core principles for everyone
- **Share the load of keeping the game focused and moving.** Whether it’s combat, exploration, or roleplay, every player helps the session stay on track and meaningful.
- **Be present even off your turn.** You may be called on for a reaction, save, or quick decision during someone else’s move, don’t zone out. Put phones away and keep one ear on the scene.
- **Let mechanics and narrative support each other.** Roleplay doesn’t replace rolls; use your stats and skills when the GM calls for them.
- **Respect the spotlight.** Don’t dominate scenes; ensure quieter players get time. Rotate focus so everyone shines.
#### Player etiquette
- **On your turn: be ready and decisive.** Know your abilities, track your resources, and act with intent when your name is called.
- **Off your turn: stay engaged.** Track initiative, plan your next move, watch for combo setups, and be ready to use reactions.
- **Support others’ moments.** If you’ve had the spotlight for a while, wrap and pass it on; when someone else is center stage, enhance their scene with short, in-character responses.
- **Don’t metagame.** Keep player knowledge separate from character knowledge; let the world surprise you.
- **Ask when unsure.** Quick, respectful questions keep play smooth; the GM will help and you don’t need every rule memorized.
- **Mind the table’s tone.** Session Zero sets expectations for content and style, play to that agreement.
#### GM etiquette
- **Facilitate fairly and consistently.** Use the Handbook as a framework, keep rulings explainable, and maintain consistency to protect immersion.
- **Listen and share spotlight.** Draw quieter players in and keep one player from dominating.
- **Keep play moving.** If a rule is unclear, make a **temporary ruling** now and finalize later; don’t change rules mid-combat unless it’s an emergency fix everyone agrees to.
- **Challenge, don’t punish.** Reward exploration and creativity; never retaliate or “win” against your players.
- **Describe like a camera.** Focus on what players would notice; avoid monologues that stall play.
- **Encourage collaboration and stay flexible.** Build situations where different play-styles rely on each other; listen and adapt as interests change.
#### Handling disagreements (without derailing the game)
- **Don’t argue mid-scene.** Keep the story moving; rules can be clarified later.
- **Use a quick ruling, then revisit.** GMs should make a provisional call and continue; finalize the detail after the encounter or session.
- **Escalate respectfully after the session.** If something still feels wrong, discuss it calmly outside the heat of play. In-game disputes stay in character; out-of-game friction is handled with clarity and care.
- **Remember roles.** The GM is guide and referee, trust that they’re aiming for fairness and momentum.
#### What to do when it isn’t your turn
- **Track the order and the scene.** Keep initiative in mind and follow the evolving fiction so you can respond instantly when prompted.
- **Prep your next move.** Know your options and resources (HP/Vitality, spells, items). Coordinate with allies for setups and defenses.
- **Stay off your phone and in the world.** Distractions make you miss triggers and slow the table down.
#### In General
- **Let others speak and take their turns.** Share spotlight and rotate focus so everyone has moments to shine.
- **Don’t argue with the GM; clarify later.** Use temporary rulings to keep pace; revisit after.
- **Stay in character; save distractions for breaks.** Immersion and focus make sessions smoother and more fun.
- **Help, don’t hijack.** Remind each other of abilities, but don’t take over someone else’s choices.




========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/2.0 Reference Tables/2.1 Affinities.md
========================================================================

Some affinities cannot be increased until reaching high enough affinity with other affinities. Most affinities have a weakness, if a creature is of a type it is usually resistant to its affinity and weak to whatever that affinity is weak to (typically). For more detailed on Affinity see [[1.3 Player Stats]] in the section titled Affinity. 

| Affinity Type | Notes                                                                                                                | Weak To                                  |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| Generic/Force | Cannot be increased with regular affinity points                                                                     | None                                     |
| Fire/Heat     | None                                                                                                                 | Water                                    |
| Earth         | None                                                                                                                 | Air                                      |
| Water         | None                                                                                                                 | Earth                                    |
| Air           | None                                                                                                                 | Fire                                     |
| Radiant       | Must have at least 3 points in any of the following affinities: Fire, Earth, Water, Air \| (3)                       | Necrotic                                 |
| Necrotic      | Must have at least 3 points in any of the following affinities: Fire, Earth, Water, Air \| (3)                       | Radiant                                  |
| Psychic       | Must have at least 3 points in Radiant + Necrotic \| (6)                                                             | Generic/Force                            |
| Lightning     | Must have at least 10 points in Fire + Air \| (20)                                                                   | Water                                    |
| Steam         | Must have at least 10 points in Fire + Water \| (20)                                                                 | Mud, Water, Earth                        |
| Magma         | Must have at least 10 points in Fire + Earth \| (20)                                                                 | Ice/Cold, Water, Air                     |
| Ice/Cold      | Must have at least 10 points in Air + Water \| (20)                                                                  | Magma, Fire, Earth                       |
| Dust          | Must have at least 10 points in Air + Earth \| (20)                                                                  | Lightning, Fire, Air                     |
| Mud           | Must have at least 10 points in Water + Earth \| (20)                                                                | Dust, Earth, Air                         |
| Nova          | Must have at least 10 points in Radiant + Fire \| (20)                                                               | Poison, Necrotic, Water                  |
| Solar         | Must have at least 10 points in Radiant + Earth \| (20)                                                              | Toxin, Necrotic, Air                     |
| Hallowed      | Must have at least 10 points in Radiant + Water \| (20)                                                              | Infernal                                 |
| Starlight     | Must have at least 10 points in Radiant + Air \| (20)                                                                | Ash, Necrotic, Fire                      |
| Cursed        | Must have at least 10 points in Radiant + Necrotic \| (20)                                                           | Infernal                                 |
| Ash           | Must have at least 10 points in Necrotic + Fire \| (20)                                                              | Hallowed, Radiant, Water                 |
| Blight        | Must have at least 10 points in Necrotic + Earth \| (20)                                                             | Starlight, Radiant, Air                  |
| Poison        | Must have at least 10 points in Necrotic + Water \| (20)                                                             | Solar, Radiant, Earth                    |
| Toxin         | Must have at least 10 points in Necrotic + Air \| (20)                                                               | Nova, Radiant, Fire                      |
| Bloodfire     | Must have at least 10 points in Psychic + Fire \| (20)                                                               | Torrent, Generic/Force, Water            |
| Tremor        | Must have at least 10 points in Psychic + Earth \| (20)                                                              | Thunder, Generic/Force, Air              |
| Deluge        | Must have at least 10 points in Psychic + Water \| (20)                                                              | Metal, Generic/Force, Earth              |
| Shatter       | Must have at least 10 points in Psychic + Air \| (20)                                                                | Infernal, Generic/Force, Fire            |
| Sorrow        | Must have at least 10 points in Psychic + Radiant \| (20)                                                            | Vacuum, Generic/Force, Necrotic          |
| Chaos         | Must have at least 10 points in Psychic + Necrotic \| (20)                                                           | Mirage, Generic/Force, Radiant           |
| Infernal      | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Fire \| (20)                                                                 | Hallowed                                 |
| Metal         | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Earth \| (20)                                                                | Air                                      |
| Torrent       | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Water \| (20)                                                                | Earth                                    |
| Thunder       | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Air \| (20)                                                                  | Fire                                     |
| Mirage        | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Radiant \| (20)                                                              | Necrotic                                 |
| Vacuum        | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Necrotic \| (20)                                                             | Radiant                                  |
| Warp          | Must have at least 10 points in Force + Psychic \| (20)                                                              | Infernal                                 |
| Storm         | Must have at least 10 points in Fire + Earth + Water + Air \| (40)                                                   | Magma, Dust, Mud, Fire, Earth,           |
| Frostfire     | Must have at least 10 points in Water + Air + Fire \| (30)                                                           | Mud, Earth, Dust                         |
| Glacial       | Must have at least 10 points in Water + Air + Necrotic \| (30)                                                       | Magma, Nova, Solar, Fire, Earth, Radiant |
| Void          | Must have at least 10 points in Water + Air + Force \| (30)                                                          | Infernal, Eldritch                       |
| Obsidian      | Must have at least 10 points in Water + Air + Earth, + Necrotic \| (40)                                              | Lightning, Nova, Solar, Starlight        |
| Quake         | Must have at least 10 points in Earth + Air + Force \| (30)                                                          | Lightning, Fire, Air                     |
| Corruption    | Must have at least 10 points in Radiant + Water + Air + Earth and 20 points in Necrotic \| (60)                      | Infernal                                 |
| Miasma        | Must have at least 10 points in Air + Water and 20 points in Necrotic \| (40)                                        | Magma, Nova, Solar, Fire, Earth, Radiant |
| Gel           | Must have at least 10 points in Water, Necrotic and 20 Points in Earth \| (40)                                       | Infernal                                 |
| Atomic        | Must have at least 10 points in Fire, Earth, Water, Air, Radiant, and Necrotic \| (60)                               | Infernal                                 |
| Eldritch      | Must have at least 50 points in Fire, Earth, Water, Air, Radiant, and Necrotic or Level 3.5 Eldritch Horror \| (300) | Infernal                                 |
> The (x) is how many total affinity points must be spent before you can spent points on that affinity.

> **Unlocking complex affinities**
>
> You want to cast Lightning spells. Lightning requires 10 Fire + 10 Air. That's 20 total affinity points in the base elements before you unlock it. Once unlocked, you can put Affinity Points directly into Lightning.
>
> **Here's a real path a Pyromancer might take:**
> - Start with 5 Fire, 5 Air (from racial bonuses and Affp at level 1).
> - Over the next few levels, put Affp into Fire and Air until both hit 10. That's 20 points total invested.
> - Now Lightning unlocks. You can put future Affp into Lightning instead of Fire/Air if you want.
>
> **What about advanced affinities like Obsidian?** That requires 10 points in Water + Air + Earth + 20 points in Necrotic (40 total). That's a major investment, you're probably level 15+ before you unlock it. And you (usually) can't shortcut it (some specific abilities and feats may directly raise affinities without first reaching the prerequisite affinity thresholds). You have to build up the tree from the roots.
> 
> When a feat or racial ability or so on grants an affinity it does not unlock that affinity in the same sense as the lightning from before. If you are able to raise it enough like that then you can use spells and features with that affinity but you cannot raise it with Affinity Points (Affp) until the prerequisites are met regardless of how high it is. 





========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/2.0 Reference Tables/2.2 Damage Types.md
========================================================================

Damage types are grouped into broad categories. Creatures may have multipliers against specific damage types or entire groupings. 

- **Immunity:** Take no damage
- **Greater Resistance:** Take 1/3 damage (round down)
- **Resistance:** Take 1/2 damage (round down)
- **Lesser Resistance:** Take 3/4 damage (round down)
- *(no multiplier — take full damage)*
- **Lesser Weakness:** Take 1.5× damage (round down)
- **Weakness:** Take 2× damage
- **Greater Weakness:** Take 3× damage

Groupings
- **Physical**: Non-magical sources (slashing, bludgeoning, piercing)
- **Elemental**: Elemental sources (fire, earth, water, air, and combinations)
- **Magical**: Magical sources (elemental damage type when from a spell specifically)
- **Mixed/Magical Physical**: Physical damage (slashing, bludgeoning, piercing) from magical sources or enchanted weapons)
- **Special**: Unusual sources (psychic, force, true)

For example, a creature with Greater Resistance to Elemental takes 1/3 damage from fire, water, lightning, magma — everything elemental. A creature with Greater Weakness to slashing takes 3× damage from any slashing weapon.

| Damage Type         | Grouping  | Effects / Notes                                                                         |
| ------------------- | --------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Slashing            | Physical  | None                                                                                    |
| Magical Slashing    | Mixed     | If dealing damage to health, DC 10 Con save, on fail target gains Bleeding condition    |
| Bludgeoning         | Physical  | None                                                                                    |
| Magical Bludgeoning | Mixed     | If dealing damage to health, DC 10 Con save, on fail target gains Suffocating condition |
| Piercing            | Physical  | None                                                                                    |
| Magical Piercing    | Mixed     | If dealing damage to health, DC 10 Con save, on fail target gains Pierced condition     |
| Fire/Heat           | Elemental | None                                                                                    |
| Earth               | Elemental | None                                                                                    |
| Water               | Elemental | Applies soaked condition                                                                |
| Air                 | Elemental | None                                                                                    |
| Radiant             | Elemental | None                                                                                    |
| Necrotic            | Elemental | None                                                                                    |
| Psychic             | Special   | Only affects Vitality; never deals Health damage.                                       |
| Force               | Special   | None                                                                                    |
| True                | Special   | Always goes to HP ignoring Vitality, cannot be resisted or immune                       |
## Combination Damage Types
Combination Damage Types are advanced damage categories formed by merging two or more elemental or magical sources into a single hybrid effect. In order to be resistant or weak to a combination damage type you must either be weak or resistant to all components or the combination type specifically. 

**Note:** If a condition causes a damage type which causes a condition or vice versa it is not recursive. 

| Name          | Formula                      | Effect / Notes                                                                                                                                                            |
| ------------- | ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Lightning     | Fire + Air                   | Deals double damage to creatures with Soaked condition                                                                                                                    |
| Steam         | Fire + Water                 | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Magma         | Fire + Earth                 | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Ice/Cold      | Air + Water                  | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Dust          | Air + Earth                  | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Mud           | Water + Earth                | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Nova          | Radiant + Fire               | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Solar         | Radiant + Earth              | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Hallowed      | Force + Radiant              | Deals triple damage to undead creatures and double damage to creatures with greater than 4 infernal affinity, when dealing damage to health target gains Purged condition |
| Starlight     | Radiant + Air                | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Cursed        | Radiant + Necrotic           | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Ash           | Necrotic + Fire              | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Blight        | Necrotic + Earth             | Deals triple damage to plants                                                                                                                                             |
| Poison        | Necrotic + Water             | If dealing damage to health, DC 15 Con save, on fail target gains Poisoned condition                                                                                      |
| Toxin         | Necrotic + Air               | If dealing damage to health, DC 15 Con save, on fail target gains Poisoned condition                                                                                      |
| Bloodfire     | Psychic + Fire               | Gain +1 health per 3 points of damage dealt                                                                                                                               |
| Tremor        | Psychic + Earth              | DC 15 Str save, on fail target gains Prone condition                                                                                                                      |
| Deluge        | Psychic + Water              | DC 15 Str save, on fail target gains Suffocating condition                                                                                                                |
| Shatter       | Psychic + Air                | DC 15 Str save, on fail target gains Demoralized condition                                                                                                                |
| Sorrow        | Psychic + Radiant            | DC 15 Str save, on fail target gains Despair condition                                                                                                                    |
| Chaos         | Psychic + Necrotic           | DC 17 Con save, on fail target gains Enraged condition                                                                                                                    |
| Infernal      | Force + Fire                 | If dealing damage to health target gains Hellfire condition                                                                                                               |
| Metal         | Force + Earth                | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Torrent       | Force + Water                | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Thunder       | Force + Air                  | DC 15 Str save, on fail target is pushed back 5 ft                                                                                                                        |
| Mirage        | Force + Radiant              | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Vacuum        | Force + Necrotic             | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Warp          | Force + Psychic              | DC 10 Con save, on fail target is forcibly moved to any unoccupied space within 10 ft of them of your choosing                                                            |
| Storm         | Lightning + Dust             | Target gains soaked effect and on DC 15 Con save fail gain Storm Shocked condition                                                                                        |
| Frostfire     | Ice/Cold + Fire              | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Glacial       | Ice/Cold + Necrotic          | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Void          | Ice/Cold + Force             | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Obsidian      | Ice/Cold + Blight            | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Quake         | Dust + Force                 | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Corruption    | Cursed + Obsidian            | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Miasma        | Toxin + Poison               | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Gel           | Mud + Blight                 | None                                                                                                                                                                      |
| Atomic        | Nova + Obsidian              | Target gains 1 stack of radiation                                                                                                                                         |
| Eldritch      | Unknown                      | Ignores immunities and resistances                                                                                                                                        |

> **Damage types at a glance: what you actually need to know**
>
> There are six groupings: Physical (non-magical weapons), Magical (spells and enchanted weapons), Elemental (fire, water, etc.), Mixed (physical enhanced with magic), and Special (psychic, force, true). Creatures can have immunity, greater resistance (1/3), resistance (1/2), weakness (2x), or greater weakness (3x) to any of these. Resistances to a grouping apply to every damage type in that group. If a creature has greater resistance to Elemental, it takes 1/3 damage from Fire, Water, Lightning, Magma; everything elemental. Same for weakness. 
>
> **Combination damage types** like Lightning (Fire + Air) use a different rule: you're only resistant or weak to them if you're resistant or weak to ALL their components OR the combination type specifically. So being resistant to Fire alone doesn't make you resistant to Lightning; you'd also need Air resistance.
>
> **Special cases to remember:**
> - Psychic only affects Vitality and never deals Health damage, but imposes powerful conditions.
> - True damage ignores everything Vitality, resistances, immunities. True is true.





========================================================================
  MARKDOWN: Eyum TTRPG/2.0 Reference Tables/2.3 Injuries.md
========================================================================

Use the damage type of the source that dealt HP damage. If a source explicitly inflicts a listed condition (such as Necrosis or Hellfire), apply that condition as written as well as the table’s default.  Conditions from injuries are applied on top of the standard conditions and checks from taking damage from that damage source. Injury conditions are applied separately from normal conditions, they do not stack with normal conditions like normal conditions would stack, they do however stack with other injury conditions. 

Each condition includes an x# value indicating how many turns it lasts. Unless otherwise specified these conditions cannot be ended early without using a spell or skill that directly ends them (Normal end/removal conditions do not apply to injuries). Otherwise, the condition automatically ends at the end of its listed duration, regardless of any other timing or effects. Some effects (like obsidian's slow death) do not have a specified x#, this means the condition continues like normal until it is removed as normal instead of waiting it out like a normal injury. Also effects like Prone can be removed as normal, having multiple stacks will mean you fall prone at the start of each turn until your stacks run out. 

| Damage Type                            | Minor                                | Moderate                             | Severe                               | Extreme                              |
| -------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
| Slashing                               | Bleeding x1                          | Bleeding x2                          | Bleeding x3                          | Bleeding x4                          |
| Magical Slashing                       | Bleeding x2                          | Bleeding x4                          | Bleeding x6                          | Bleeding x8                          |
| Bludgeoning                            | Suffocating x1                       | Suffocating x2                       | Suffocating x3                       | Suffocating x4                       |
| Magical Bludgeoning                    | Suffocating x2                       | Suffocating x4                       | Suffocating x6                       | Suffocating x8                       |
| Piercing                               | Bleeding x1                          | Bleeding x2                          | Bleeding x3                          | Bleeding x4                          |
| Magical Piercing                       | Bleeding x2                          | Bleeding x4                          | Bleeding x6                          | Bleeding x8                          |
